rules.rule_statement: In California, the rules governing the legal profession are principally found in the Rules of Professional Conduct (CA Rules or CA RPC), and in the State Bar Act (SBA), located in the California Business and Professions Code. The CA Rules govern the activities of California lawyers practicing both within and outside California. The CA Rules also regulate the activities of lawyers from other jurisdictions when they perform lawyer functions in California. Nothing contained in the CA Rules authorizes the performance of attorney functions by persons in California except as otherwise permitted by law. Division 3, Chapter 4, Sections 6000 et seq. CA RPC Rule 8.5.
rules.short_rule_statement: In California, the rules governing the legal profession are principally found in the Rules of Professional Conduct (CA Rules or CA RPC), and in the State Bar Act (SBA), located...
rules.source_text:
California State Rules
In California, the rules governing the legal profession are principally found in the Rules of Professional Conduct (CA Rules or CA RPC), and in the State Bar Act (SBA), located in the California Business and Professions Code. The CA Rules govern the activities of California lawyers practicing both within and outside California. The CA Rules also regulate the activities of lawyers from other jurisdictions when they perform lawyer functions in California. Nothing contained in the CA Rules authorizes the performance of attorney functions by persons in California except as otherwise permitted by law. Division 3, Chapter 4, Sections 6000 et seq. CA RPC Rule 8.5.

rules.rule_statement: This outline includes coverage of the Model Rules of Professional Conduct (Model Rules or MR) promulgated by the American Bar Association (ABA) in 1983 and amended several times since then. The Model Rules consist of specific rules with explanatory comments. This outline references the 2002 version of the Model Rules. California has not adopted the Model Rules of Professional Conduct in their entirety but has modeled the CA Rules after the order and organization of the Model Rules. On the California Bar Exam, you must be able to address situations in which the CA RPC and the Model Rules differ.
rules.short_rule_statement: This outline includes coverage of the Model Rules of Professional Conduct (Model Rules or MR) promulgated by the American Bar Association (ABA) in 1983 and amended several...
rules.source_text:
Model Rules Of Professional Conduct
This outline includes coverage of the Model Rules of Professional Conduct (Model Rules or MR) promulgated by the American Bar Association (ABA) in 1983 and amended several times since then. The Model Rules consist of specific rules with explanatory comments. This outline references the 2002 version of the Model Rules. California has not adopted the Model Rules of Professional Conduct in their entirety but has modeled the CA Rules after the order and organization of the Model Rules. On the California Bar Exam, you must be able to address situations in which the CA RPC and the Model Rules differ.

rules.rule_statement: The CA RPC and the SBA govern the conduct of lawyers in their relationships with their clients in an attempt to protect the integrity of the judicial system. All lawyers are subject to discipline under the authority of the State Bar of California pursuant to the California Business and Professions Code, the California Rules of Court and, implicitly, the CA RPC. In representing a client, a lawyer must exercise independent, professional judgment and render candid advice. CA RPC Rule 1.0. MR 2.1; CA RPC Rule 2.1.
rules.short_rule_statement: The CA RPC and the SBA govern the conduct of lawyers in their relationships with their clients in an attempt to protect the integrity of the judicial system. All lawyers are...
rules.source_text:
Lawyer-Client Relationship
The CA RPC and the SBA govern the conduct of lawyers in their relationships with their clients in an attempt to protect the integrity of the judicial system. All lawyers are subject to discipline under the authority of the State Bar of California pursuant to the California Business and Professions Code, the California Rules of Court and, implicitly, the CA RPC. In representing a client, a lawyer must exercise independent, professional judgment and render candid advice. CA RPC Rule 1.0. MR 2.1; CA RPC Rule 2.1.

rules.rule_statement: A lawyer generally has no duty to accept representation of any client. However, a lawyer has a duty to accept court appointments and to perform a certain number of hours of pro bono service annually, so long as no good cause exists to decline.
rules.short_rule_statement: A lawyer generally has no duty to accept representation of any client. However, a lawyer has a duty to accept court appointments and to perform a certain number of hours of pro...
rules.source_text:
Acceptance Or Rejection Of Clients
A lawyer generally has no duty to accept representation of any client. However, a lawyer has a duty to accept court appointments and to perform a certain number of hours of pro bono service annually, so long as no good cause exists to decline.

rules.rule_statement: Good cause to decline representation exists if: The lawyer could not handle the matter competently; ii. The representation would result in an improper conflict of interest (e.g., when the client or the cause is so repugnant to the lawyer that it is likely to impair the lawyer’s ability to zealously represent the client); or iii. Acceptance would be unreasonably burdensome (e.g., when it would impose a financial sacrifice so great as to be unjust). An appointed lawyer has the same obligations to the client as retained counsel, including the obligations of loyalty and confidentiality, and the lawyer is subject to the same limitations regarding the lawyer-client relationship, including the obligation to refrain from assisting the client in violation of the Model Rules.
rules.short_rule_statement: Good cause to decline representation exists if: The lawyer could not handle the matter competently; ii. The representation would result in an improper conflict of interest...
rules.source_text:
Declining Court Appointments
Good cause to decline representation exists if: The lawyer could not handle the matter competently; ii. The representation would result in an improper conflict of interest (e.g., when the client or the cause is so repugnant to the lawyer that it is likely to impair the lawyer’s ability to zealously represent the client); or iii. Acceptance would be unreasonably burdensome (e.g., when it would impose a financial sacrifice so great as to be unjust). An appointed lawyer has the same obligations to the client as retained counsel, including the obligations of loyalty and confidentiality, and the lawyer is subject to the same limitations regarding the lawyer-client relationship, including the obligation to refrain from assisting the client in violation of the Model Rules.

rules.rule_statement: A lawyer must not: iv. Bring an action, conduct a defense, assert a position in litigation, or take an appeal without probable cause and for the purpose of harassing or maliciously injuring any person; or Present a claim or defense in litigation that is not warranted under existing law, unless it can be supported by a good-faith argument seeking an extension, modification, or reversal of the existing law.

Prohibited Objectives Of Employment
A lawyer must not: iv. Bring an action, conduct a defense, assert a position in litigation, or take an appeal without probable cause and for the purpose of harassing or maliciously injuring any person; or Present a claim or defense in litigation that is not warranted under existing law, unless it can be supported by a good-faith argument seeking an extension, modification, or reversal of the existing law.

rules.rule_statement: Once a lawyer undertakes representation, the full range of obligations and duties to a client exist. At that point, the lawyer is both a fiduciary and an agent of the client.
rules.short_rule_statement: Once a lawyer undertakes representation, the full range of obligations and duties to a client exist. At that point, the lawyer is both a fiduciary and an agent of the client.
rules.source_text:
Undertaking Representation
Once a lawyer undertakes representation, the full range of obligations and duties to a client exist. At that point, the lawyer is both a fiduciary and an agent of the client.

rules.rule_statement: The lawyer-client relationship begins when the client reasonably believes the relationship exists. No formal writing or agreement is required. EXAM NOTE: Look for casual conversations between a lawyer and a potential client.  If the facts indicate that the client reasonably believes the relationship exists, it does.
rules.short_rule_statement: The lawyer-client relationship begins when the client reasonably believes the relationship exists. No formal writing or agreement is required. EXAM NOTE: Look for casual...
rules.source_text:
Reasonable Belief Of Representation
The lawyer-client relationship begins when the client reasonably believes the relationship exists. No formal writing or agreement is required. EXAM NOTE: Look for casual conversations between a lawyer and a potential client.  If the facts indicate that the client reasonably believes the relationship exists, it does.

rules.rule_statement: A lawyer has a duty to reject representation when accepting the representation would violate a rule of ethics or law or when the lawyer’s physical or mental condition materially impairs the lawyer’s ability to represent the client. A lawyer should not accept representation unless it can be performed competently, promptly, without improper conflict of interest, and to completion. In California, a lawyer has a duty never to reject, for personal reasons, the cause of the defenseless or the oppressed. MR 6.2. MR 3.1; CA RPC Rule 3.1(a). MR 1.16(a)(1), (2); CA RPC Rule 1.16. SBA § 6068(h).
rules.short_rule_statement: A lawyer has a duty to reject representation when accepting the representation would violate a rule of ethics or law or when the lawyer’s physical or mental condition...
rules.source_text:
Duty To Reject
A lawyer has a duty to reject representation when accepting the representation would violate a rule of ethics or law or when the lawyer’s physical or mental condition materially impairs the lawyer’s ability to represent the client. A lawyer should not accept representation unless it can be performed competently, promptly, without improper conflict of interest, and to completion. In California, a lawyer has a duty never to reject, for personal reasons, the cause of the defenseless or the oppressed. MR 6.2. MR 3.1; CA RPC Rule 3.1(a). MR 1.16(a)(1), (2); CA RPC Rule 1.16. SBA § 6068(h).

rules.canonical_name: Scope, Objective, And Means Of The Representation
rules.rule_statement: Rule The client determines the objectives of the representation, but the attorney decides the means to reach the client’s objectives based on consultation with the client. The Model Rules and the CA RPC provide the client with the ultimate authority to determine the purposes to be served by legal representation within the limits imposed by the law and the lawyer’s professional obligations. As the client’s agent, the lawyer must abide by those decisions (within some constraints) but has the authority to determine how best to achieve those objectives.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule The client determines the objectives of the representation, but the attorney decides the means to reach the client’s objectives based on consultation with the client. The...
rules.source_text:
Scope, Objective, And Means Of The Representation
Rule The client determines the objectives of the representation, but the attorney decides the means to reach the client’s objectives based on consultation with the client. The Model Rules and the CA RPC provide the client with the ultimate authority to determine the purposes to be served by legal representation within the limits imposed by the law and the lawyer’s professional obligations. As the client’s agent, the lawyer must abide by those decisions (within some constraints) but has the authority to determine how best to achieve those objectives.
topic_path: Scope, Objective, And Means Of The Representation

rules.rule_statement: The lawyer and client can negotiate the scope of the representation, including the duration (e.g., through the first appeal) or the subject matter (e.g., only the tax aspects of a real-estate transaction) of the relationship. A client has no constitutional right to compel his lawyer to assert issues if the lawyer decides not to press those issues as a matter of the lawyer’s professional judgment.

rules.source_text:
Scope Of Representation
The lawyer and client can negotiate the scope of the representation, including the duration (e.g., through the first appeal) or the subject matter (e.g., only the tax aspects of a real-estate transaction) of the relationship. A client has no constitutional right to compel his lawyer to assert issues if the lawyer decides not to press those issues as a matter of the lawyer’s professional judgment.
topic_path: Scope, Objective, And Means Of The Representation

rules.rule_statement: Rule The lawyer must communicate offers of settlement to the client, but the final decision whether to accept any offer rests with the client. This duty applies to a written settlement offer or an oral offer in a civil matter if it is a significant development. A lawyer who agrees to a settlement without the client’s consent and authorization is subject to discipline, but the opposing party may enforce the agreement if the lawyer acted with apparent authority. EXAM NOTE: The requirement to communicate settlement offers to the client has been commonly tested on the California Bar Exam.

Acceptance Of Settlement
Rule The lawyer must communicate offers of settlement to the client, but the final decision whether to accept any offer rests with the client. This duty applies to a written settlement offer or an oral offer in a civil matter if it is a significant development. A lawyer who agrees to a settlement without the client’s consent and authorization is subject to discipline, but the opposing party may enforce the agreement if the lawyer acted with apparent authority. EXAM NOTE: The requirement to communicate settlement offers to the client has been commonly tested on the California Bar Exam.

rules.rule_statement: A client in a criminal case decides: Whether to testify at trial; ii. Whether to waive the right to a jury trial or other fundamental rights; and iii. Whether to enter a plea.
rules.short_rule_statement: A client in a criminal case decides: Whether to testify at trial; ii. Whether to waive the right to a jury trial or other fundamental rights; and iii. Whether to enter a plea.
rules.source_text:
Criminal Cases
A client in a criminal case decides: Whether to testify at trial; ii. Whether to waive the right to a jury trial or other fundamental rights; and iii. Whether to enter a plea.
topic_path: Scope, Objective, And Means Of The Representation

rules.rule_statement: The lawyer has the authority to make most decisions relating to the strategy and methods for achieving the client’s goals (e.g., the manner and scope of cross-examination, choosing a theory of the case). The lawyer must reasonably consult with the client about the means to accomplish the client’s objectives and to keep the client reasonably informed about the status of the matter. If a disagreement between the lawyer and the client cannot be resolved, the lawyer may withdraw or the client may fire the lawyer.

Procedural Tactics
The lawyer has the authority to make most decisions relating to the strategy and methods for achieving the client’s goals (e.g., the manner and scope of cross-examination, choosing a theory of the case). The lawyer must reasonably consult with the client about the means to accomplish the client’s objectives and to keep the client reasonably informed about the status of the matter. If a disagreement between the lawyer and the client cannot be resolved, the lawyer may withdraw or the client may fire the lawyer.

rules.rule_statement: A lawyer may limit the scope of the representation if: iv. The limitation is reasonable under the circumstances, The limitation is not otherwise prohibited by law, and vi. The client provides informed consent. The lawyer and client may agree to exclude or limit specific means that might otherwise be used, including actions that are too costly for the client or that the lawyer regards as repugnant or imprudent.

The client provides informed consent. The lawyer and client may agree to exclude or limit specific means that might otherwise be used, including actions that are too costly for the client or that the lawyer regards as repugnant or imprudent.

rules.rule_statement: To the extent possible, a lawyer representing a client with diminished capacity (due to mental impairment, age, or other factors) must maintain an ordinary lawyer-client relationship. The CA RPC do not include a provision regarding clients with diminished capacity.
rules.short_rule_statement: To the extent possible, a lawyer representing a client with diminished capacity (due to mental impairment, age, or other factors) must maintain an ordinary lawyer-client...
rules.source_text:
Client Under Disability
To the extent possible, a lawyer representing a client with diminished capacity (due to mental impairment, age, or other factors) must maintain an ordinary lawyer-client relationship. The CA RPC do not include a provision regarding clients with diminished capacity.
topic_path: Scope, Objective, And Means Of The Representation

rules.canonical_name: Protective Action, Including Appointment Of A Guardian
rules.rule_statement: A lawyer may take reasonably necessary protective action when a lawyer reasonably believes that the client has diminished capacity, is at risk of substantial physical, financial, or other harm unless action is taken, and cannot adequately act in his own interest. In appropriate cases, the lawyer may seek appointment of a guardian, conservator, or guardian ad litem to act on behalf of the client.

rules.source_text:
Protective Action, Including Appointment Of A Guardian
A lawyer may take reasonably necessary protective action when a lawyer reasonably believes that the client has diminished capacity, is at risk of substantial physical, financial, or other harm unless action is taken, and cannot adequately act in his own interest. In appropriate cases, the lawyer may seek appointment of a guardian, conservator, or guardian ad litem to act on behalf of the client.
topic_path: Scope, Objective, And Means Of The Representation
Protective Action, Including Appointment Of A Guardian

rules.canonical_name: Revealing Conﬁdential Information About A Client Under Disability
rules.rule_statement: In general, unless authorized to do so, the lawyer may not disclose information relating to the representation of a client with diminished capacity. When taking protective action, including seeking the appointment of a guardian, however, the lawyer is impliedly authorized to reveal information about the client, even if the client directs otherwise. The information may be revealed only to the extent reasonably necessary to protect the client’s interests. The lawyer should determine whether it is likely that the person or entity consulted will act adversely to the client’s interests before discussing matters related to the client.
rules.short_rule_statement: In general, unless authorized to do so, the lawyer may not disclose information relating to the representation of a client with diminished capacity. When taking protective...
rules.source_text:
Revealing Conﬁdential Information About A Client Under Disability
In general, unless authorized to do so, the lawyer may not disclose information relating to the representation of a client with diminished capacity. When taking protective action, including seeking the appointment of a guardian, however, the lawyer is impliedly authorized to reveal information about the client, even if the client directs otherwise. The information may be revealed only to the extent reasonably necessary to protect the client’s interests. The lawyer should determine whether it is likely that the person or entity consulted will act adversely to the client’s interests before discussing matters related to the client.

rules.rule_statement: During emergency situations in which the health, safety, or financial interest of a person with seriously diminished capacity is threatened with imminent and irreparable harm, a lawyer may take legal action on the client’s behalf. The lawyer may act even if the person is unable to establish a lawyer-client relationship, or to make or express considered judgments about the matter, when that person or another person acting in good faith on his behalf has consulted with the lawyer. However, the lawyer should not act unless he reasonably believes that the person has no lawyer, agent, or other representative available. The lawyer should take legal action only to the extent reasonably necessary to maintain the status quo or otherwise avoid imminent and irreparable harm. The lawyer will have the same duties he would have to any client, including the duty of confidentiality, and should not normally seek compensation for emergency actions taken for a nonclient.
rules.short_rule_statement: During emergency situations in which the health, safety, or financial interest of a person with seriously diminished capacity is threatened with imminent and irreparable harm,...
rules.source_text:
Emergency Legal Assistance
During emergency situations in which the health, safety, or financial interest of a person with seriously diminished capacity is threatened with imminent and irreparable harm, a lawyer may take legal action on the client’s behalf. The lawyer may act even if the person is unable to establish a lawyer-client relationship, or to make or express considered judgments about the matter, when that person or another person acting in good faith on his behalf has consulted with the lawyer. However, the lawyer should not act unless he reasonably believes that the person has no lawyer, agent, or other representative available. The lawyer should take legal action only to the extent reasonably necessary to maintain the status quo or otherwise avoid imminent and irreparable harm. The lawyer will have the same duties he would have to any client, including the duty of confidentiality, and should not normally seek compensation for emergency actions taken for a nonclient.

rules.canonical_name: Prohibition On Counseling Crimes Or Fraud
rules.rule_statement: Rule A lawyer is prohibited from counseling or assisting a client in conduct that the lawyer knows to be criminal, fraudulent, or a violation of any law, rule, or ruling of a tribunal. If the lawyer advises or assists with such a violation, then the lawyer is subject to both discipline and criminal or civil liability. However, a lawyer may discuss the legal consequences of any proposed course of conduct with a client and may counsel or assist the client to make a good-faith effort to determine the validity, scope, meaning, or application of the law, rule, or ruling of a tribunal. If the client’s course of action has already begun and is continuing, then the lawyer is required to avoid assisting the client and must withdraw from the representation. In certain cases, withdrawal alone may be insufficient, and the lawyer may have to give notice of the withdrawal and publicly disaffirm any opinion, document, or affirmation previously provided to the client. MR 1.2, cmt. 1; CA RPC Rule 1.2, cmt. 1. MR 1.2(a); CA RPC Rule 1.2(a). Jones v. Barnes, 463 U.S. 745 (1983). MR 1.2(a); CA RPC Rules 1.2(a), 1.4.1(a). MR 1.2(a); CA RPC Rule 1.2(a). MR 1.2(a); CA RPC Rule 1.2(a). See MR 1.16, infra. MR 1.2(c); CA RPC Rule 1.2(b). MR 1.2, cmt. 6. MR 1.14(a). MR 1.6. MR 1.2(d); CA RPC Rule 1.2.1.

rules.short_rule_statement: A lawyer must give the client reasonable notice before withdrawing so the client has a reasonable opportunity to obtain new representation.

Notice
A lawyer must give the client reasonable notice before withdrawing so the client has a reasonable opportunity to obtain new representation.

rules.rule_statement: A lawyer must obtain permission before termination if required by the rules of a tribunal.
rules.short_rule_statement: A lawyer must obtain permission before termination if required by the rules of a tribunal.
rules.source_text:
Court Approval
A lawyer must obtain permission before termination if required by the rules of a tribunal.

rules.rule_statement: A lawyer attempting to withdraw must continue to represent the client if ordered to do so by a court, even if there is otherwise good cause for terminating the representation.
rules.short_rule_statement: A lawyer attempting to withdraw must continue to represent the client if ordered to do so by a court, even if there is otherwise good cause for terminating the representation.
rules.source_text:
Court Order To Continue
A lawyer attempting to withdraw must continue to represent the client if ordered to do so by a court, even if there is otherwise good cause for terminating the representation.

rules.rule_statement: Rule Upon termination, the lawyer has a duty to take reasonable measures to minimize the harm done to the client. Even if the lawyer was unfairly discharged by the client, the lawyer must take all reasonable steps to mitigate the consequences to the client.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule Upon termination, the lawyer has a duty to take reasonable measures to minimize the harm done to the client. Even if the lawyer was unfairly discharged by the client, the...
rules.source_text:
Lawyer’S Duties Upon Termination
Rule Upon termination, the lawyer has a duty to take reasonable measures to minimize the harm done to the client. Even if the lawyer was unfairly discharged by the client, the lawyer must take all reasonable steps to mitigate the consequences to the client.

rules.rule_statement: Rules Regarding Returning Client Papers and Property The lawyer must return all papers and property “to which the client is entitled.” The lawyer may retain papers relating to the client as security for a fee only to the extent permitted by law.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rules Regarding Returning Client Papers and Property The lawyer must return all papers and property “to which the client is entitled.” The lawyer may retain papers relating to...
rules.source_text:
Returning Client Papers And Property
Rules Regarding Returning Client Papers and Property The lawyer must return all papers and property “to which the client is entitled.” The lawyer may retain papers relating to the client as security for a fee only to the extent permitted by law.

rules.rule_statement: Subject to any protective order or nondisclosure agreement, the lawyer must promptly release to the client, at his request, all client papers and property, including: (1) correspondence,(2) pleadings, (3) deposition transcripts, (4) exhibits,(5) physical evidence, (6) experts’ reports and other writings, and (7) other items reasonably necessary to the client’s representation, regardless of whether the client has paid for them. Statutes may prohibit a lawyer from releasing information and property under certain circumstances. In certain criminal matters, a lawyer may be required to retain a copy of a former client’s file for the term of the client’s imprisonment.

Subject to any protective order or nondisclosure agreement, the lawyer must promptly release to the client, at his request, all client papers and property, including: (1) correspondence,(2) pleadings, (3) deposition transcripts, (4) exhibits,(5) physical evidence, (6) experts’ reports and other writings, and (7) other items reasonably necessary to the client’s representation, regardless of whether the client has paid for them. Statutes may prohibit a lawyer from releasing information and property under certain circumstances. In certain criminal matters, a lawyer may be required to retain a copy of a former client’s file for the term of the client’s imprisonment.

rules.rule_statement: Upon termination, the lawyer must promptly refund any unearned portion of a fee paid in advance. This rule does not apply to a true retainer fee, which is paid solely for ensuring the availability of the lawyer for the matter. If a client disputes the amount to be returned, then the lawyer must comply with the rules regarding disputed property.

rules.rule_statement: A lawyer must charge fees that are reasonable under the circumstances. Fees are regulated for reasonableness based on the amount and nature of the fees. Only contingent-fee arrangements must be in writing, but all rate or fee arrangements must be communicated to the client before, or within a reasonable time after, the relationship commences. EXAM NOTE: Whenever a lawyer’s fee is mentioned in a fact pattern, be sure to consider the reasonableness of the fee.
rules.short_rule_statement: A lawyer must charge fees that are reasonable under the circumstances. Fees are regulated for reasonableness based on the amount and nature of the fees. Only contingent-fee...
rules.source_text:
Reasonable Fee
A lawyer must charge fees that are reasonable under the circumstances. Fees are regulated for reasonableness based on the amount and nature of the fees. Only contingent-fee arrangements must be in writing, but all rate or fee arrangements must be communicated to the client before, or within a reasonable time after, the relationship commences. EXAM NOTE: Whenever a lawyer’s fee is mentioned in a fact pattern, be sure to consider the reasonableness of the fee.

rules.rule_statement: Factors Considered in Determining the Reasonableness of Attorney’s Fees Difficulty of the case Factors reflecting the difficulty of the case may be considered in evaluating reasonableness, such as the time, labor, novelty, and skill necessary to perform the services, as well as the time limitations imposed by the client or the specific circumstances surrounding the case. Preclusion of other The likelihood that the representation will preclude other employment employment, either because of the time requirements or potential conflicts, may be considered, but only if the client is aware of these limitations. Nature of the relationship The way in which the particular lawyer-client relationship was and the fee arrangement developed or evolved over time, as well as whether the fee is fixed, contingent, prepaid, or hourly may be considered. Expertise of the attorney A seasoned, well-known attorney may charge more for the same service than a new attorney without specific expertise. Fee charged in a locality The customary fee charged for similar services in a given locality may also be considered.

rules.rule_statement: California prohibits illegal or unconscionable fees. Unconscionability is determined based on all the facts and circumstances existing at the time the agreement is entered into, except when the parties contemplate that the fee will be affected by later events. Factors considered in determining whether a fee is unconscionable include: Whether the lawyer engaged in fraud or overreaching in negotiating or setting the fee; ii. Whether the lawyer has failed to disclose material facts; iii. The amount of the fee in proportion to the value of the services performed; iv. The relative sophistication of the lawyer and the client; The novelty and difficulty of the questions involved and the skill needed to perform the legal services properly; vi. The likelihood, if apparent to the client, that acceptance of particular employment will preclude other employment by the lawyer; vii. The amount involved and the results obtained; viii. Time limitations imposed by the client or by the circumstances; ix. The nature and length of the professional relationship with the client; The experience, reputation, and ability of the lawyer or lawyers performing the services; xi. Whether the fee is fixed or contingent; xii. The time and labor required; and xiii. Whether the client gave informed consent to the fee. EXAM NOTE: The reasonableness of fees and the California unconscionability standard have been tested frequently on the California Bar Exam.

rules.rule_statement: ii) Calculation methodology of the fee; and iii) Details of the calculation for expense deductions, including whether those expenses are to be deducted before or after the contingency fee is calculated. The agreement must clearly outline any expenses for which the client will be liable regardless of whether the client is the prevailing party. The lawyer must provide a written statement explaining the outcome at the conclusion of a contingent-fee matter. If the client has prevailed and recovered damages, then the statement must include the amount paid to the client and the method used to determine that amount. A lawyer who represents a client on a contingent-fee basis must, at the time of contract formation, provide a duplicate copy of the contract, signed by both the lawyer and the client, or by the client’s guardian or representative, to the client, his guardian, or his representative. The contract must be in writing and must include: A statement of the agreed upon contingency-fee rate; ii. A statement as to how disbursements and costs will affect the contingency fee and the client’s recovery; iii. A statement regarding the extent, if any, to which the client could be required to compensate the lawyer for related matters arising out of their relationship, but not covered by the contingent-fee contract, which may include any amounts collected for the plaintiff by the lawyer; iv) If the claim is subject to California’s medical malpractice fee limits, a statement that the statutory limits are the maximum permitted in the contingent-fee agreement and that the lawyer and the client may negotiate a lower rate; and iv. If the claim is not subject to the statutory medical malpractice fees, a statement that the fee is not set by law but is negotiable between the lawyer and the client.

rules.rule_statement: A lawyer may require advance payment as long as the lawyer returns any unearned portion upon termination. Payment In Advance Versus Retainer
A lawyer may require advance payment as long as the lawyer returns any unearned portion upon termination. If the lawyer charges a retainer (i.e., money paid solely to ensure the availability of the lawyer), then the lawyer generally does not have to refund the fee if the representation ends, unless the parties agree otherwise.

Property is an acceptable form of payment as long as it is not in the form of a proprietary interest in the cause of action or subject matter of the litigation and is in compliance with MR 1.8 on business transactions with clients.

rules.rule_statement: A lawyer may not make an agreement with a client that might induce the lawyer to improperly curtail services for the client or perform them in a way contrary to the client’s interest. Therefore, a lawyer may not enter into an agreement under which services are to be provided only up to a stated amount when it is foreseeable that more extensive services will likely be required, unless the situation is adequately explained to the client. Otherwise, the client might have to bargain for further assistance in the midst of a proceeding or transaction. However, it is proper to define the extent of services in light of the client’s ability to pay.

rules.source_text:
No Agreement To Curtail Services
A lawyer may not make an agreement with a client that might induce the lawyer to improperly curtail services for the client or perform them in a way contrary to the client’s interest. Therefore, a lawyer may not enter into an agreement under which services are to be provided only up to a stated amount when it is foreseeable that more extensive services will likely be required, unless the situation is adequately explained to the client. Otherwise, the client might have to bargain for further assistance in the midst of a proceeding or transaction. However, it is proper to define the extent of services in light of the client’s ability to pay.

rules.rule_statement: The Model Rules generally prohibit payment to anyone for recommending a lawyer’s services, unless paying the fees of a legal services plan or approved lawyer-referral service. A referral fee or forwarding fee in return for merely recommending another attorney violates this rule. A lawyer who performs a service for the client before the referral (e.g., interviewing the client) may be reasonably compensated for the service. The Model Rules allow for formalized referral agreements. In California, a lawyer generally must not compensate, promise, or give anything of value to a person for the purpose of recommending or securing the services of the lawyer or the lawyer's law firm. However, a lawyer may offer or give a gift or gratuity to a person who made a recommendation resulting in the employment of the lawyer or the lawyer's law firm, provided that the gift or gratuity was not offered or given in consideration of any promise, agreement, or understanding that the gift or gratuity would be forthcoming or additional referrals would be made or encouraged in the future. A lawyer is permitted to pay the usual charges of a legal services plan or a qualified lawyer referral service approved by the State Bar of California's Minimum Standards for a Lawyer Referral Service in California. Referral fees paid to another lawyer are permitted, so long as they meet the requirements for fee splitting described supra.

rules.rule_statement: Fees may not be shared and a partnership may not be made with nonlawyers if any of the activities includes the practice of law. Limited exceptions to this rule occur if: xiv. Fees are paid into a lawyer’s estate or to one or more specified persons over a reasonable period as a death benefit according to the firm’s operating agreement; xv. A lawyer who purchases the practice of a deceased, disabled, or missing lawyer pays the agreed-upon purchase price to the estate or other representative of that lawyer; xvi. Fees are shared with law firm personnel through a compensation or retirement plan, even though the plan is based on a profit-sharing arrangement; or xvii. A lawyer shares court-awarded legal fees with a nonprofit organization that employed, retained, or recommended the lawyer’s employment in the matter. The CA RPC also permit a lawyer or law firm to share fees in two additional circumstances: xviii. A lawyer or law firm may share with or pay a prescribed registration, referral, or other fee to a lawyer referral service that is established, sponsored, and operated in accordance with the State Bar of California’s Minimum Standards for Lawyer Referral Services. xix. A lawyer or law firm may share with or pay a legal fee that arises from a settlement or other resolution of the matter with a nonprofit organization that employed, retained, recommended, or facilitated employment of the lawyer or law firm in the matter, provided that (1) there is a written agreement to divide the fee with the nonprofit organization, (2) the lawyer or law firm obtains the client’s consent in writing after full written disclosure, and (3) the total fee charged is not increased solely because of the agreement to divide fees.

rules.rule_statement: Under certain circumstances, a lawyer may agree to refer clients to another lawyer or to a nonlawyer professional in return for the undertaking of that person to refer clients or customers to the lawyer. This is known as a reciprocal referral agreement. The Model Rules and the CA RPC require that (1) the reciprocal referral agreement not be exclusive and (2) the client be informed of the existence and nature of the agreement. Additionally, the agreement must not interfere with the lawyer’s professional judgment as to making referrals or as to providing substantive legal services. A reciprocal referral agreement should not be of indefinite duration and should be reviewed periodically to determine whether it complies with the ethics rules.
rules.short_rule_statement: Under certain circumstances, a lawyer may agree to refer clients to another lawyer or to a nonlawyer professional in return for the undertaking of that person to refer clients...
rules.source_text:
Reciprocal Referral Agreements
Under certain circumstances, a lawyer may agree to refer clients to another lawyer or to a nonlawyer professional in return for the undertaking of that person to refer clients or customers to the lawyer. This is known as a reciprocal referral agreement. The Model Rules and the CA RPC require that (1) the reciprocal referral agreement not be exclusive and (2) the client be informed of the existence and nature of the agreement. Additionally, the agreement must not interfere with the lawyer’s professional judgment as to making referrals or as to providing substantive legal services. A reciprocal referral agreement should not be of indefinite duration and should be reviewed periodically to determine whether it complies with the ethics rules.

rules.rule_statement: Minimum fee schedules once imposed on attorneys are no longer lawful because their anticompetitive nature violates the Sherman Antitrust Act. Therefore, they are not enforceable. By statute, California imposes maximum fee schedules for certain types of legal work, including medical malpractice claims.
rules.short_rule_statement: Minimum fee schedules once imposed on attorneys are no longer lawful because their anticompetitive nature violates the Sherman Antitrust Act. Therefore, they are not...
rules.source_text:
Minimum And Maximum Fee Schedules
Minimum fee schedules once imposed on attorneys are no longer lawful because their anticompetitive nature violates the Sherman Antitrust Act. Therefore, they are not enforceable. By statute, California imposes maximum fee schedules for certain types of legal work, including medical malpractice claims.

rules.rule_statement: In California, all funds received or held for the benefit of clients by a lawyer or law firm, including advances for costs and expenses, must be deposited in one or more identifiable bank accounts labeled “Trust Account,” “Client’s Funds Account,” or something similar. The account(s) must be maintained in California or, with the written consent of the client, in any other jurisdiction where there is a substantial relationship between the client or the client’s business and the other jurisdiction. However, a flat fee paid in advance for legal services may be deposited in a lawyer’s or law firm’s operating account, provided the lawyer or law firm discloses to the client in writing that (1) the client has a right to require that the flat fee be deposited in a trust account until it is earned and (2) the client is entitled to a refund of any amount of the fee that has not been earned if the representation is terminated or the services have not been completed. If the flat fee exceeds $1,000, the client’s agreement to deposit the flat fee in the lawyer’s operating account must be in a writing signed by the client. The California State Bar has implemented a proactive regulation program for the handling of entrusted funds and property received or held by the lawyer called the Client Trust Account Protection Program ("CTAPP"). The program is designed to increase public protection in the area of a lawyer’s handling of entrusted funds and property. One of the goals of this program is to promote compliance and avoid misconduct before a client is harmed by mismanagement of entrusted funds or property. Under the program, lawyers who are responsible for complying with any of the obligations under CA RPC 1.15 concerning client trust funds must report to the State Bar and register each account. The lawyers must complete an annual self-assessment questionnaire about the duties and practices for properly handling client funds and certify compliance with all rules and statutes governing client trust accounts and safekeeping client funds. Failure to comply with the program results in fees for noncompliance and enrollment as involuntarily inactive.

rules.source_text:
In General
In California, all funds received or held for the benefit of clients by a lawyer or law firm, including advances for costs and expenses, must be deposited in one or more identifiable bank accounts labeled “Trust Account,” “Client’s Funds Account,” or something similar. The account(s) must be maintained in California or, with the written consent of the client, in any other jurisdiction where there is a substantial relationship between the client or the client’s business and the other jurisdiction. However, a flat fee paid in advance for legal services may be deposited in a lawyer’s or law firm’s operating account, provided the lawyer or law firm discloses to the client in writing that (1) the client has a right to require that the flat fee be deposited in a trust account until it is earned and (2) the client is entitled to a refund of any amount of the fee that has not been earned if the representation is terminated or the services have not been completed. rules.rule_statement: Rule Lawyers must keep client property and funds separate from their own and are subject to discipline for commingling funds. No funds belonging to a lawyer or law firm may be deposited in a client trust account or otherwise commingled with client funds, except for funds reasonably sufficient to pay bank charges.

No Commingling Of Funds

No funds belonging to a lawyer or law firm may be deposited in a client trust account or otherwise commingled with client funds, except for funds reasonably sufficient to pay bank charges.

An attorney must maintain an appropriate ledger with a record of the balances, deposits, expenses, and property held, among other things.

rules.rule_statement: Any portion of funds in the client trust account belonging to the lawyer must be withdrawn at the earliest reasonable time after the lawyer’s interest in that portion becomes fixed. If the client disputes the lawyer’s right to receive a portion of the trust funds, then the disputed portion must not be withdrawn until the dispute is resolved.

rules.rule_statement: Rules for Client Notice When a lawyer receives funds or other property in which a client or third person has an interest, the lawyer must promptly notify the client or third person.

rules.source_text:
Notice And Delivery
Rules for Client Notice When a lawyer receives funds or other property in which a client or third person has an interest, the lawyer must promptly notify the client or third person.

rules.rule_statement: A lawyer must, absent good cause, notify a client or third person who has an interest no later than 14 days of the receipt of the funds or other property in which the lawyer knows or reasonably should know the client or third person has an interest. A lawyer must promptly deliver to the client or third person any funds or other property that the client or third person is entitled to receive (i.e., undisputed funds) and, upon request by the client or third person, must promptly render a full accounting. Under the CA RPC, there is a rebuttable presumption that a lawyer has violated the CA RPC if the lawyer, absent good cause, fails to distribute undisputed funds or other property within 45 days of the date when the funds or other property became undisputed. Funds or other property are "undisputed" if the lawyer knows or reasonably should know that the ownership interest of the client or third person in the funds or property, or any portion thereof, has become fixed and there are no unresolved disputes as to the client's or third person's entitlement to receive the funds or property. This presumption does not apply if the lawyer and the client or third person agree in writing that the funds or other property should continue to be held by the lawyer. The presumption may be rebutted by proof by a preponderance of evidence that there was good cause for not distributing the funds or property within 45 days of the date when the funds or property became undisputed. Upon rebuttal, a violation of this rule must be established by clear and convincing evidence. See MR 1.5(a). CA RPC Rule 1.5(a). SBA § 6148(a). SBA § 6148(d). See SBA § 6147(a). SBA § 6148(a). MR 1.5(d); CA RPC Rule 1.5(c). MR 1.5(c). SBA § 6147(a). SBA §§ 6147(c), 6147.5. MR 1.5, cmt. 4; MR 1.16(d). MR 1.5, cmt. 5. See MR 1.5(e); CA RPC Rule 1.5.1. MR 1.5(e). See II.D.4.f. Reciprocal referral agreements, infra CA RPC Rule 7.2(b)(5). CA RPC Rule 7.2(b)(2). MR 5.4; CA RPC Rule 5.4. MR 5.4(a); CA RPC Rule 5.4(a). CA RPC Rule 5.4(a)(3). CA RPC Rule 5.4(a)(6). MR 7.2(b)(4); CA RPC Rule 7.2(b)(4). Goldfarb v. Va. State Bar, 421 U.S. 773 (1975). SBA §§ 6146–6147. CA RPC Rule 1.15(a). CA RPC Rule 1.15(b). MR 1.15(a). CA RPC Rule 1.15(c)(1). MR 1.15(a); CA RPC Rule 1.15(d). MR 1.15(e); CA RPC Rule 1.15(c)(2). MR 1.15(d). CA RPC Rule 1.15(d)(1). MR 1.15(d); CA RPC Rule 1.15(d)(7). CA RPC Rule 1.15(f), (g). CA RPC Rule 1.15(f). CA RPC Rule 1.15, comment 5.

rules.rule_statement: The principle of lawyer-client confidentiality is given effect by related areas of the law: (1) lawyer-client privilege, (2) the work-product rule, and (3) the ethical duty of confidentiality. The lawyer-client privilege and work-product rule apply in judicial and other legal proceedings in which a lawyer may be called as a witness or otherwise required to produce evidence regarding a client. The duty of lawyer-client confidentiality applies in situations other than those in which evidence is sought from the lawyer through legal compulsion. It applies not only to matters communicated in confidence by a client, but also to all information relating to the representation of the client, from any source. A lawyer may not disclose the information except as authorized or required by the applicable ethics rules or by other law. Be certain to understand the difference between the duty of confidentiality and the evidentiary lawyer-client privilege. Remember that only client-driven confidential communication is covered by the privilege. The duty of confidentiality also covers thirdparty communications, the lawyer’s own observations, and all information relating to the representation.

Duty Versus Privilege In General
The principle of lawyer-client confidentiality is given effect by related areas of the law: (1) lawyer-client privilege, (2) the work-product rule, and (3) the ethical duty of confidentiality. The lawyer-client privilege and work-product rule apply in judicial and other legal proceedings in which a lawyer may be called as a witness or otherwise required to produce evidence regarding a client. The duty of lawyer-client confidentiality applies in situations other than those in which evidence is sought from the lawyer through legal compulsion. It applies not only to matters communicated in confidence by a client, but also to all information relating to the representation of the client, from any source. A lawyer may not disclose the information except as authorized or required by the applicable ethics rules or by other law. Be certain to understand the difference between the duty of confidentiality and the evidentiary lawyer-client privilege. Remember that

rules.rule_statement: Rule Confidential communication between a client and his lawyer is privileged. The evidentiary lawyer-client privilege covers the client’s communication to a lawyer whom the client reasonably believes represents the client and only when the circumstances indicate the client’s desire for confidentiality. Elements of the Lawyer-Client Privilege Confidential The communication must be intended to be confidential. Communication made in the knowing presence of a third party generally destroys privilege, but the presence of, or communication by or through, a representative does not destroy the privilege. Communication The communication must be for seeking legal advice or representation, but the lawyer is not required to give advice or agree to the representation for the privilege to exist. Client-held The client holds the privilege and is the only one who may waive it. The lawyer must assert the privilege on the client’s behalf to protect the client’s interests. Duration Unless the privilege is waived, it continues indefinitely, even after the representation terminates. The privilege generally survives the client’s death. In California, it terminates when the client’s estate is settled and the personal representative is discharged.

rules.rule_statement: California recognizes no privilege regarding communication relevant to an issue between parties all of whom have filed claims through a deceased client, regardless of whether the claims are by testate or intestate succession or by inter vivos transaction. No privilege applies regarding communication relevant to an issue concerning the intention or competence of a client executing an attested document of which the lawyer is an attesting witness, or concerning the execution or attestation of such a document. A communication relevant to an issue concerning the intention of a client, now deceased, regarding a deed of conveyance, will, or other writing executed by the client purporting to affect an interest in property is not privileged in California. No privilege attaches to communication relevant to an issue concerning the validity of a deed of conveyance, will, or other writing executed by a client, now deceased, purporting to affect an interest in property.

A communication relevant to an issue concerning the intention of a client, now deceased, regarding a deed of conveyance, will, or other writing executed by the client purporting to affect an interest in property is not privileged in California. No privilege attaches to communication relevant to an issue concerning the validity of a deed of conveyance, will, or other writing executed by a client, now deceased, purporting to affect an interest in property.

rules.rule_statement: Rule In California, a writing that reflects a lawyer’s impressions, conclusions, opinions, or legal research or theories is not discoverable under any circumstance. Other lawyer work product is discoverable only if the court determines that denial of discovery will unfairly prejudice the party seeking discovery in preparing that party’s claim or defense or will result in an injustice. When a lawyer is suspected of knowingly participating in a crime or fraud, there is no work-product protection in any official investigation by a law-enforcement agency or proceeding brought by a California public prosecutor if the lawyer’s services were sought or obtained to enable or aid anyone to commit or plan a crime or fraud. In an action between a lawyer and a client or a former client, no work-product privilege exists if the work product at issue is relevant to a breach of a duty to the client arising out of the lawyer-client relationship. See Cal. Evid. Code § 954. See Cal. Evid. Code § 952.

rules.rule_statement: Under the Model Rules, a lawyer is prohibited from disclosing information relating to the representation of a client, unless: The client gives informed consent; ii. The disclosure is impliedly authorized to carry out the representation; or iii. Other specific exceptions apply. The ethical duty of confidentiality applies not only to confidential client communications, but also to all information relating to the representation, regardless of the source, including lawyer observations and third-party communications. The prohibition also applies to disclosures by a lawyer that do not reveal protected information but could reasonably lead to the discovery of such information by a third person. A lawyer may use a hypothetical to discuss issues relating to the representation so long as there is no reasonable likelihood that the listener will be able to ascertain the identity of the client or the situation involved. EXAM NOTE: Note that legal strategies about the representation would be covered by both the ethical duty of conﬁdentiality and the work-product rule.

rules.rule_statement: A prospective client under the Model Rules is a person who discusses the possibility of forming a lawyer-client relationship regarding a matter. A prospective client under the CA RPC is a person who, directly or through an authorized representative, consults a lawyer for the purpose of retaining the lawyer or securing legal service or advice from the lawyer in the lawyer’s professional capacity. Under the CA RPC, communications are not protected if made: iv. By a person who consults a lawyer without a good faith intention to retain the lawyer; After the lawyer states that she is unwilling or unable to represent the person; or vi. Unilaterally without a reasonable expectation that the lawyer will be retained.

rules.rule_statement: When transmitting a communication that includes information relating to a client’s representation, the lawyer must take reasonable precautions to prevent the information from coming into the hands of unintended recipients. This duty does not require the use of special security measures if the communication method affords a reasonable expectation of privacy. A client may require the lawyer to implement special security measures not otherwise required or may give informed consent to the use of a means of communication that would otherwise be prohibited.

rules.rule_statement: Lawyers in a firm may, in the course of the firm’s practice, disclose to each other information relating to a client of the firm, unless the client has instructed that that particular information be confined to specified lawyers. Disclosure may also be made to nonlawyers in a firm when necessary to carry out the representation. The firm must have procedures in place (including training) to ensure that nonlawyers do not violate the duty of confidentiality. Commercial Standard Title Co., Inc. v. Super. Ct. of San Diego County., 92 Cal. App. 3d 934 (1979). MR 5.3; CA RPC Rule 5.3.

rules.canonical_name: Comparison Of Model Rules And California Rule
rules.rule_statement: The Model Rules allow disclosure to prevent “reasonably certain” death or substantial bodily harm. Harm or death is reasonably certain if it will occur imminently or if there is a present and substantial threat that a person will suffer such harm or death at a later date if the lawyer fails to take action necessary to eliminate the threat. Under the Model Rules, substantial bodily harm or death need not be imminent but merely reasonably certain to occur if the lawyer does not disclose. California allows disclosure reasonably believed necessary to prevent a criminal act that the lawyer reasonably believes will result in death or substantial bodily harm. Disclosure must be no more than is necessary to prevent the criminal act, given the information known to the lawyer at the time of the disclosure. The California exception is not triggered unless the lawyer is preventing a criminal act reasonably believed to result in death or substantial bodily harm.
rules.short_rule_statement: The Model Rules allow disclosure to prevent “reasonably certain” death or substantial bodily harm. Harm or death is reasonably certain if it will occur imminently or if there...
rules.source_text:
Comparison Of Model Rules And California Rule
The Model Rules allow disclosure to prevent “reasonably certain” death or substantial bodily harm. Harm or death is reasonably certain if it will occur imminently or if there is a present and substantial threat that a person will suffer such harm or death at a later date if the lawyer fails to take action necessary to eliminate the threat. Under the Model Rules, substantial bodily harm or death need not be imminent but merely reasonably certain to occur if the lawyer does not disclose. California allows disclosure reasonably believed necessary to prevent a criminal act that the lawyer reasonably believes will result in death or substantial bodily harm. Disclosure must be no more than is necessary to prevent the criminal act, given the information known to the lawyer at the time of the disclosure. The California exception is not triggered unless the lawyer is preventing a criminal act reasonably believed to result in death or substantial bod

rules.canonical_name: Applicable No Matter Who Causes The Harm
rules.rule_statement: This exception applies even if a third party and not the client causes the potential harm or death.
rules.short_rule_statement: This exception applies even if a third party and not the client causes the potential harm or death.

Applicable No Matter Who Causes The Harm
This exception applies even if a third party and not the client causes the potential harm or death.

rules.rule_statement: The rules allow, but do not require, disclosure under these circumstances. Example: Cassie Client has hired Arthur Attorney to represent her in a heated estate dispute. Cassie tells Arthur that she intends to commit murder the next day, and Arthur believes that Cassie will carry out this threat. It would be proper for Arthur to disclose Cassie’s intentions to the authorities because there is a present and substantial threat that Cassie will cause significant bodily harm or death if Arthur fails to act. However, Arthur is not required to make this disclosure.

The Lawyer Has Discretion
The rules allow, but do not require, disclosure under these circumstances. Example: Cassie Client has hired Arthur Attorney to represent her in a heated estate dispute. Cassie tells Arthur that she intends to commit murder the next day, and Arthur believes that Cassie will carry out this threat. It would be proper for Arthur to disclose Cassie’s intentions to the authorities because there is a present and substantial threat that Cassie will cause significant bodily harm or death if Arthur fails to act. However, Arthur is not required to make this disclosure.

rules.rule_statement: Before revealing this confidential information, the lawyer must, if it is reasonable under the circumstances, make a good-faith effort to persuade the client not to commit or to continue the criminal act, or to pursue a course of conduct that will prevent the threatened death or substantial bodily harm. The lawyer must also, if reasonable under the circumstances, inform the client at an appropriate time of the lawyer’s ability or decision to reveal the information. EXAM NOTE: Recall that a lawyer may not instruct a client on how to violate the law.  Additionally, a lawyer must not accept or continue representation if the lawyer knows that the objective of the representation is unlawful (unless the lawyer believes in good faith that the law is unlawful).  A lawyer can be subject to discipline for such continued representation.
rules.short_rule_statement: Before revealing this confidential information, the lawyer must, if it is reasonable under the circumstances, make a good-faith effort to persuade the client not to commit or...
rules.source_text:
California Requirements Before Revealing
Before revealing this confidential information, the lawyer must, if it is reasonable under the circumstances, make a good-faith effort to persuade the client not to commit or to continue the criminal act, or to pursue a course of conduct that will prevent the threatened death or substantial bodily harm. The lawyer must also, if reasonable under the circumstances, inform the client at an appropriate time of the lawyer’s ability or decision to reveal the information. EXAM NOTE: Recall that a lawyer may not instruct a client on how to violate the law.  Additionally, a lawyer must not accept or continue representation if the lawyer knows that the objective of the representation is unlawful (unless the lawyer believes in good faith that the law is unlawful).  A lawyer can be subject to discipline for such continued representation.

rules.rule_statement: Under the Model Rules, a lawyer may reveal confidential information concerning the representation to the extent that the lawyer reasonably believes it is necessary to prevent the client from committing a crime or fraud that is reasonably certain to result in substantial injury to the financial interests or property of another and in furtherance of which the client has used or is using the lawyer’s services. Disclosure is also permitted to prevent, mitigate, or rectify substantial injury to the financial interests or property of another that is reasonably certain to result or has resulted from the client’s commission of a crime or fraud in furtherance of which the client has used the lawyer’s services. EXAM NOTE: These exceptions are not applicable under the CA RPC or SBA. This distinction is commonly tested on the California Bar Exam.
rules.short_rule_statement: Under the Model Rules, a lawyer may reveal confidential information concerning the representation to the extent that the lawyer reasonably believes it is necessary to prevent...
rules.source_text:
Substantial Financial Harm To Another
Under the Model Rules, a lawyer may reveal confidential information concerning the representation to the extent that the lawyer reasonably believes it is necessary to prevent the client from committing a crime or fraud that is reasonably certain to result in substantial injury to the financial interests or property of another and in furtherance of which the client has used or is using the lawyer’s services. Disclosure is also permitted to prevent, mitigate, or rectify substantial injury to the financial interests or property of another that is reasonably certain to result or has resulted from the client’s commission of a crime or fraud in furtherance of which the client has used the lawyer’s services. EXAM NOTE: These exceptions are not applicable under the CA RPC or SBA. This distinction is commonly tested on the California Bar Exam.

rules.rule_statement: The lawyer may use discretion regarding disclosure, but the lawyer may not, under any circumstances, counsel or assist the client in conduct that the lawyer knows is criminal or fraudulent.
rules.short_rule_statement: The lawyer may use discretion regarding disclosure, but the lawyer may not, under any circumstances, counsel or assist the client in conduct that the lawyer knows is criminal...

Discretionary With The Lawyer
The lawyer may use discretion regarding disclosure, but the lawyer may not, under any circumstances, counsel or assist the client in conduct that the lawyer knows is criminal or fraudulent.

rules.canonical_name: Person Committing Fraud Then Employs A Lawyer
rules.rule_statement: The exceptions do not apply when a person who has committed a crime or fraud later employs a lawyer for representation concerning that offense.
rules.short_rule_statement: The exceptions do not apply when a person who has committed a crime or fraud later employs a lawyer for representation concerning that offense.

rules.canonical_name: Securing Legal Advice About A Lawyer’S Compliance
rules.rule_statement: A lawyer’s duty of confidentiality does not prohibit her from securing confidential legal advice about her own compliance with the Model Rules. Generally, disclosing information to obtain that advice would be impliedly authorized for the lawyer to carry out the representation. Even if not impliedly authorized, the Model Rules allow such disclosure.

Even if not impliedly authorized, the Model Rules allow such disclosure.

rules.canonical_name: Controversy Between A Lawyer And A Client
rules.rule_statement: Under the Model Rules, a lawyer may reveal confidential information concerning the representation to the extent that she reasonably believes it necessary to: Establish a claim or defense on her behalf in a controversy between herself and the client; ii. Establish a defense to a criminal charge or civil claim against her based on conduct in which the client was involved; or iii. Respond to allegations in any proceeding concerning the representation of the client. Example: Client disputes a fee owed to Lawyer for representation in a criminal defense matter. Lawyer is permitted to prove the services rendered in the defense action to collect the fee.
rules.short_rule_statement: Under the Model Rules, a lawyer may reveal confidential information concerning the representation to the extent that she reasonably believes it necessary to: Establish a claim...

rules.canonical_name: Compliance With Other Laws Or A Court Order
rules.rule_statement: Under the Model Rules, a lawyer may reveal confidential information concerning the representation to the extent that he reasonably believes it is necessary to comply with other laws or a court order. Unless the client provides informed consent to do otherwise, the lawyer should assert on behalf of the client all nonfrivolous claims that the order is not authorized by other laws or that the information sought is protected against disclosure by the lawyer-client privilege or other applicable law. MR 1.6(b)(1). CA RPC Rule 1.6(b). CA RPC Rule 1.6(d). CA RPC Rule 1.6(c). MR 1.6(b)(2). MR 1.6(b)(3). MR 1.6, cmt. 9.

rules.rule_statement: Lawyers owe clients a basic duty of loyalty and independent professional judgment. When a lawyer’s independent professional judgment is potentially compromised by a nonclient interest (i.e., a conflict of interest exists), the lawyer may be in breach of the duty of loyalty. A conflict of interest may arise between the lawyer and the client, between the interests of current clients or current and prospective clients or current and former clients, or between current clients and third parties. MR 1.7, cmt. 1; CA RPC Rules 1.7, 1.8.1.
rules.short_rule_statement: Lawyers owe clients a basic duty of loyalty and independent professional judgment. When a lawyer’s independent professional judgment is potentially compromised by a nonclient...
rules.source_text:
Independent Professional Judgment-Conflicts Of Interest
Lawyers owe clients a basic duty of loyalty and independent professional judgment. When a lawyer’s independent professional judgment is potentially compromised by a nonclient interest (i.e., a conflict of interest exists), the lawyer may be in breach of the duty of loyalty. A conflict of interest may arise between the lawyer and the client, between the interests of current clients or current and prospective clients or current and former clients, or between current clients and third parties. MR 1.7, cmt. 1; CA RPC Rules 1.7, 1.8.1.

rules.rule_statement: A lawyer must not represent a client if there is a significant risk that the representation may be materially limited by the lawyer’s own interests, unless: The lawyer reasonably believes that he will be able to provide competent and diligent representation to the affected client; ii. The representation is not prohibited by law; iii. The representation does not involve the assertion of a claim by one client against another client represented by the lawyer in the same litigation or other proceeding before a tribunal; and iv. The affected client gives informed consent, confirmed in writing (MR) or informed written consent (CA). EXAM NOTE: The Model Rules use the phrase “informed consent conﬁrmed in writing,” while the CA RPC use the phrase “informed written consent.”  Informed written consent requires written disclosure of the potential adverse consequences of the client consenting to a conﬂicted representation.  Be sure to use the proper phrase associated with each set of rules in your essay answers. Both the specific lawyer’s subjective belief and a disinterested lawyer’s reasonable belief are required in the analysis of whether the representation would be materially limited by the lawyer’s own interests.

rules.rule_statement: A lawyer may not represent a client in a matter in which the lawyer is related to the lawyer for the opposing party unless each client gives informed consent. Disqualification arising from a close family relationship is personal and ordinarily not imputed to members of firms with whom the lawyers are associated.

A lawyer may not represent a client in a matter in which the lawyer is related to the lawyer for the opposing party unless each client gives informed consent. Disqualification arising from a close family relationship is personal and ordinarily not imputed to members of firms with whom the lawyers are associated.
topic_path: As Affected By Lawyer'S Personal Interest

rules.rule_statement: In California, even when there is not a significant risk that the lawyer’s representation of a client will be materially limited, a lawyer is not permitted to represent a client in a matter in which the lawyer knows or reasonably should know that another party’s lawyer is a spouse, parent, child, or sibling of the member, lives with the lawyer, is a client of the lawyer, or has an intimate personal relationship with the lawyer, unless the lawyer provides written disclosure of the relationship to the client.

topic_path: As Affected By Lawyer'S Personal Interest

rules.rule_statement: The Model Rules and the CA RPC prohibit a lawyer from engaging in sexual relations with a client, unless a consensual sexual relationship existed between the lawyer and client when the lawyer-client relationship commenced. The California rule includes an express exception for sexual relations with a client who is the lawyer’s spouse or registered domestic partner. The rule does not permit the client to waive the conflict through informed consent. The conflict is personal to the lawyer and is not imputed to others in the lawyer’s firm. If the client is an organization, then the lawyer for the organization (whether in-house or outside counsel) is prohibited from having a sexual relationship with a constituent of the organization (e.g., an employee of a corporation) who supervises, directs, or regularly consults with the lawyer with regard to the organization’s legal matters.
rules.short_rule_statement: The Model Rules and the CA RPC prohibit a lawyer from engaging in sexual relations with a client, unless a consensual sexual relationship existed between the lawyer and client...

Sexual Relations With A Client
The Model Rules and the CA RPC prohibit a lawyer from engaging in sexual relations with a client, unless a consensual sexual relationship existed between the lawyer and client when the lawyer-client relationship commenced. The California rule includes an express exception for sexual relations with a client who is the lawyer’s spouse or registered domestic partner. The rule does not permit the client to waive the conflict through informed consent. The conflict is personal to the lawyer and is not imputed to others in the lawyer’s firm. If the client is an organization, then the lawyer for the organization (whether in-house or outside counsel) is prohibited from having a sexual relationship with a constituent of the organization (e.g., an employee of a corporation) who supervises, directs, or regularly consults with the lawyer with regard to the organization’s legal matters.

rules.canonical_name: Relationship Or Responsibility To A Party Or Witness
rules.rule_statement: In California, even when there is not a significant risk that the lawyer’s representation of a client will be materially limited, a lawyer is not permitted to represent a client in a matter in which the lawyer has, or knows that another lawyer in the lawyer’s firm has, a legal, business, financial, professional, or personal relationship with or responsibility to a party or witness in the same matter, unless the lawyer first informs the client in writing of the relationship. MR 1.7(b); CA RPC Rule 1.7(b), (d). MR 1.7, cmt. 11. CA RPC Rule 1.7(c)(2). MR 1.8(j); CA RPC Rule 1.8.10. MR 1.8(k); CA RPC Rule 1.8.11. CA RPC Rule 1.7(c)(1).

MR 1.7(b); CA RPC Rule 1.7(b), (d). MR 1.7, cmt. 11. CA RPC Rule 1.7(c)(2). MR 1.8(j); CA RPC Rule 1.8.10. MR 1.8(k); CA RPC Rule 1.8.11. CA RPC Rule 1.7(c)(1).

rules.rule_statement: Due to a potential conflict between the interests of a client and those of the lawyer in testifying truthfully, a lawyer may be disqualified from representing a client if the lawyer is likely to be a necessary witness in the client’s action.
rules.short_rule_statement: Due to a potential conflict between the interests of a client and those of the lawyer in testifying truthfully, a lawyer may be disqualified from representing a client if the...
rules.source_text:
Lawyer As Witness
Due to a potential conflict between the interests of a client and those of the lawyer in testifying truthfully, a lawyer may be disqualified from representing a client if the lawyer is likely to be a necessary witness in the client’s action.

rules.rule_statement: A lawyer is not permitted to act as an advocate at a trial in which the lawyer is likely to be a necessary witness unless: The testimony relates to an uncontested issue; ii. The testimony relates to the nature and value of legal services rendered in the case; or iii. Disqualification of the lawyer would result in substantial hardship to the client.

rules.rule_statement: In California, a lawyer is not permitted to act as an advocate at a trial in which the lawyer is likely to be a witness unless: iv. The testimony relates to an uncontested matter; The testimony relates to the nature and value of legal services rendered in the case; or vi. The lawyer has obtained the client’s informed written consent. If the lawyer represents the state or a governmental entity, then consent must be obtained from the head of the office or a designee of the head of the office by which the lawyer is employed and must be consistent with principles of recusal.

rules.rule_statement: A lawyer is permitted to act as an advocate in a trial in which another lawyer in the lawyer’s firm is likely to be called as a witness, unless the conflict-of-interest rules preclude the lawyer from doing so. MR 3.7. CA RPC Rule 3.7(a). MR 3.7(b); CA RPC Rule 3.7(b).
