rules.canonical_name: Proprietary Interest In Causes Of Action
rules.rule_statement: Under the Model Rules, a lawyer must not obtain a proprietary interest in the cause of action or subject matter of litigation in which a client is represented, except when the lawyer acquires a lien granted by law to secure payment of a fee; or the lawyer contracts for a reasonable contingent fee, provided the case is not criminal or a domestic-relations matter.
rules.short_rule_statement: Under the Model Rules, a lawyer must not obtain a proprietary interest in the cause of action or subject matter of litigation in which a client is represented, except when the...
rules.source_text:
Proprietary Interest In Causes Of Action
Under the Model Rules, a lawyer must not obtain a proprietary interest in the cause of action or subject matter of litigation in which a client is represented, except when the lawyer acquires a lien granted by law to secure payment of a fee; or the lawyer contracts for a reasonable contingent fee, provided the case is not criminal or a domestic-relations matter.
topic_path: Proprietary Interest In Causes Of Action

rules.rule_statement: The Model Rules prohibit financial assistance to a client with respect to pending or planned litigation, except that a lawyer is permitted to advance litigation costs (including the expenses of medical examination and the costs of obtaining and presenting evidence). Although the client usually must remain ultimately liable for those costs, repayment may be made contingent on the outcome of the case. Lawyers representing indigent clients may advance court costs and litigation expenses regardless of whether the funds will be repaid. In addition, a lawyer representing an indigent client pro bono may provide modest gifts to the client for food, rent, transportation, medicine, and other basic living expenses. However, the lawyer may not: Promise, assure, or imply the availability of those gifts prior to retention or as an inducement to continue the client-lawyer relationship after retention; ii. Seek or accept reimbursement from the client, a relative of the client, or anyone affiliated with the client; and iii. Publicize or advertise a willingness to provide those gifts to prospective clients.
rules.short_rule_statement: The Model Rules prohibit financial assistance to a client with respect to pending or planned litigation, except that a lawyer is permitted to advance litigation costs...

Seek or accept reimbursement from the client, a relative of the client, or anyone af

rules.rule_statement: Rule In California, a lawyer is not permitted to directly or indirectly pay or agree to pay the personal or business expenses of a prospective or existing client. Therefore, while the Model Rules limit financial assistance only in the context of litigation, the CA RPC limit financial assistance generally. A lawyer is permitted, with the client’s consent, to pay or agree to pay those expenses to third persons from funds collected or to be collected for the client as a result of the representation. The lawyer may also, after being hired, lend money to the client if the client promises in writing to repay the loan. Additionally, a lawyer may advance the costs of prosecuting or defending a claim or action or otherwise protecting or promoting the client’s interests, the repayment of which may be contingent on the outcome of the matter, or the interests of an indigent person. Costs may include reasonable expenses of litigation or reasonable expenses in preparation for litigation or in providing legal services. MR 1.8(i). MR 1.8(e). CA RPC Rule 1.8.5.

The lawyer may also, after being hired, lend money to the client if the client promises in writing to repay the loan. rules.rule_statement: Rule A lawyer must not enter into a business transaction with a client or knowingly acquire any interest adverse to a client, unless the terms are fully disclosed in writing, they are fair and reasonable to the client, and the client understands them. The client must be advised in writing of the desirability of seeking independent counsel and be given an opportunity to do so. The client must consent in writing to the transaction and its terms after a full written disclosure of the terms and the lawyer’s role in the transaction (i.e., informed written consent). Standard commercial transactions between the lawyer and the client for products or services that the client generally markets to others (e.g., banking or brokerage services, medical services, products manufactured or distributed by the client, and utilities’ services) are exempt from this rule.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule A lawyer must not enter into a business transaction with a client or knowingly acquire any interest adverse to a client, unless the terms are fully disclosed in writing,...

Business Transactions With A Client
Rule A lawyer must not enter into a business transaction with a client or knowingly acquire any interest adverse to a client, unless the terms are fully disclosed in writing, they are fair and reasonable to the client, and the client understands them. The client must be advised in writing of the desirability of seeking independent counsel and be given an opportunity to do so. The client must consent in writing to the transaction and its terms after a full written disclosure of the terms and the lawyer’s role in the transaction (i.e., informed written consent). Standard commercial transactions between the lawyer and the client for products or services that the client generally markets to others (e.g., banking or brokerage services, medical services, products manufactured or distributed by the client, and utilities’ services) are exempt from this rule.

rules.rule_statement: Under the Model Rules, a lawyer is prohibited from negotiating for literary or media rights relating to representation of a client before the conclusion of the representation. The Model Rules do not prohibit a lawyer representing a client in a transaction concerning literary property from agreeing that the lawyer’s fee will consist of a share in ownership in the property, if the arrangement does not result in an unreasonable fee and meets the rules regarding business transactions with a client.

The Model Rules do not prohibit a lawyer representing a client in a transaction concerning literary property from agreeing that the lawyer’s fee will consist of a share in ownership in the property, if the arrangement does not result in an unreasonable fee and meets the rules regarding business transactions with a client.

rules.rule_statement: California has no specific ethics rule prohibiting negotiating for literary or media rights, though CA RPC Rule 1.8.1, discussed supra, dealing with business transactions with a client, would be applicable. In criminal cases, there is a danger that a conflict of interest will prejudice a defendant’s right to effective assistance of counsel under the Sixth Amendment. California courts have found that any conflict can be overcome by the client’s informed written consent.

In criminal cases, there is a danger that a conflict of interest will prejudice a defendant’s right to effective assistance of counsel under the Sixth Amendment. California courts have found that any conflict can be overcome by the client’s informed written consent.

Unless the lawyer is related to the client, the lawyer may not solicit or prepare an instrument that gives a substantial gift to the lawyer or a person related to the lawyer (such as in a will, trust agreement, or deed).

rules.rule_statement: In addition to permitting a lawyer to prepare an instrument that gives a substantial gift to the lawyer or a person related to the lawyer if the lawyer is related to the client, the CA RPC also allow a lawyer to prepare such an instrument if the client has been advised by an independent lawyer, even if the client is not related to the lawyer who prepares the instrument. Related persons include persons related by blood or affinity. A lawyer may accept an unsolicited gift from a client if the transaction meets general standards of fairness (e.g., a present given at a holiday or as a token of appreciation). If a client offers the lawyer a more substantial gift, then the rule does not prohibit the lawyer from accepting it, but such a gift may be voidable by the client under the doctrine of undue influence, which treats client gifts as presumptively fraudulent.

rules.rule_statement: A lawyer must not represent a client if doing so would be directly adverse to the interests of another current client or if there is a significant risk that the representation of the client will be materially limited by the lawyer’s responsibilities to the current client, unless: The lawyer reasonably believes that she will be able to provide competent and diligent representation to each affected client; ii. The representation is not prohibited by law; iii. The representation does not involve the assertion of a claim by one client against another client represented by the lawyer in the same litigation or other proceeding before a tribunal; and iv. Each affected client gives informed consent, confirmed in writing (MR) or informed written consent (CA). Under no circumstances may a lawyer represent opposing parties in the same lawsuit or transaction. Absent informed consent in writing, a lawyer also may not act as an advocate in one matter against a person the lawyer represents in some other matter, even when the matters are wholly unrelated. Directly adverse conflicts can also arise in transactional matters. Therefore, if a lawyer is asked to represent the seller of a business in negotiations with a buyer represented by the lawyer in another unrelated matter, then the lawyer could not undertake the representation without the informed written consent of each client. However, simultaneous representation in unrelated matters of clients whose interests are only economically adverse, such as representation of competing economic enterprises (e.g., two baseball teams) in unrelated litigation, does not ordinarily constitute a conflict of interest and therefore may not require consent of the respective clients. Even if clients are not directly adverse, a conflict of interest will exist if there is a significant risk that a lawyer’s ability to consider, recommend, or carry out an appropriate course of action for the client will be materially limited as a result of the lawyer’s other responsibilities or interests. Therefore, if a lawyer is asked to represent several persons seeking to form a partnership, then the lawyer is likely to be materially limited in his ability to recommend or advocate all possible positions that each partner might take because of the lawyer’s duty of loyalty to the others. But the mere possibility of subsequent harm does not require disclosure and consent. The lawyer must evaluate the likelihood that a difference in interests will occur and whether it will materially interfere with the lawyer’s independent, professional judgment in considering alternatives that reasonably should be pursued on behalf of the client. The specific lawyer’s subjective belief and a disinterested lawyer’s reasonable belief are each required in the analysis of whether the representation would be adverse or materially limited by the lawyer’s responsibilities to another client.

rules.rule_statement: Rule A lawyer who previously represented a client in a matter must not subsequently represent another person in the same or a substantially related matter in which that person’s interests are materially adverse to the interests of the former client, unless the former client gives informed consent, confirmed in writing. The California rule specifies “informed written consent.” The scope of a matter for the purposes of determining a conflict depends on the facts of each particular situation or transaction. If a lawyer was directly involved in a specific transaction, then subsequent representation of other clients with materially adverse interests in that transaction is clearly prohibited. If the lawyer commonly handled a type of problem for a former client, then the lawyer is not prohibited from later representing another client in a factually different problem of the same type, even though the subsequent representation involves a position adverse to the prior client. Substantially related matters involve the same transaction or legal dispute or involve substantial risk that confidential factual information normally obtained in the prior representation would materially advance the new client’s position in the subsequent matter. Therefore, a lawyer who represented a businessperson and learned extensive private financial information about the person may not then represent that person’s spouse in a divorce. Information previously disclosed to the public or to other parties adverse to the former client generally will not cause disqualification. A lawyer who has formerly represented a client or whose present or former firm has formerly represented a client in a matter may not use information relating to the representation to the disadvantage of the former client, except when the ethics rules (and in California, the SBA) allow confidential information to be revealed or when the information becomes generally known. The former client can waive this impediment through informed consent, confirmed in writing. The California rule specifies “informed written consent.”

rules.rule_statement: Any conflict of interest that arises from the lawyer’s association with a prior law firm will not be imputed to the lawyer’s new firm if: The disqualified lawyer is timely screened from any participation in the matter and is apportioned no part of the fee; ii. Written notice is given to any affected former client to enable the former client to ascertain compliance with ethical rules; and iii) Certification of compliance with ethical rules and with screening procedures is provided to the former client by the screened lawyer and by a partner of the firm at reasonable intervals upon the former client’s written request and upon termination of the screening procedures.

Model Rules
Any conflict of interest that arises from the lawyer’s association with a prior law firm will not be imputed to the lawyer’s new firm if: The disqualified lawyer is timely screened from any participation in the matter and is apportioned no part of the fee; ii. Written notice is given to any affected former client to enable the former client to ascertain compliance with ethical rules; and iii) Certification of compliance with ethical rules and with screening procedures is provided to the former client by the screened lawyer and by a partner of the firm at reasonable intervals upon the former client’s written request and upon termination of the screening procedures.

rules.rule_statement: The California Rules are more restrictive and permit screening only in limited circumstances. Any conflict of interest that arises from the lawyer’s association with a prior law firm will not be imputed to the lawyer’s new firm if: The prohibited lawyer did not substantially participate in the same or a substantially related matter; ii. The disqualified lawyer is timely screened from any participation in the matter and is apportioned no part of the fee; and iii) Written notice is given to any affected former client to enable the former client to ascertain compliance with the ethics rules. If a lawyer has terminated an association with a firm, then the firm is not prohibited from subsequently representing a person with interests materially adverse to those of a client represented by the formerly associated lawyer and not currently represented by the firm, unless (1) the matter is the same or substantially related to that in which the formerly associated lawyer represented the client, and (2) any lawyer remaining in the firm has confidential information that is material to the matter. A client may give informed consent, in writing, to overcome a disqualification. The California rule specifies “informed written consent.”

rules.rule_statement: The Model Rules and the CA RPC prohibit a former government lawyer from representing a client in a matter in which the lawyer participated personally and substantially as a government lawyer, unless the appropriate government agency gives its informed consent, confirmed in writing, to the representation. The California rule specifies “informed written consent.” The prohibition applies regardless of whether the lawyer is adverse to the government in attempting to represent the new client. Therefore, a lawyer who has pursued a claim on behalf of the government may not pursue the same claim on behalf of a subsequent private client after the lawyer has left government service, except when the government agency gives its informed written consent. If a former government lawyer is disqualified from representation, then no lawyer in his firm may knowingly undertake or continue representation in such a matter, unless: fee; and ii) Written notice is promptly given to the appropriate government agency, allowing the agency to determine whether the lawyer and firm are in compliance with the conflict rules. Screening requires the isolation of the lawyer from any participation in the matter through the timely imposition of procedures within a firm that are reasonably adequate under the circumstances to protect information that the isolated lawyer is obligated to protect. California rules permit similar screening procedures.

rules.rule_statement: Except as the law expressly permits, a government lawyer who acquires known confidential government information about a person may not later represent a private client whose interests are adverse to that person in a matter in which the information could be used to that person’s material disadvantage. “Confidential government information” means information obtained under governmental authority and which, at the time the rule is applied, the government is prohibited from disclosing to the public or has a legal privilege not to disclose and which is not otherwise available to the public.

Except as the law expressly permits, a government lawyer who acquires known confidential government information about a person may not later represent a private client whose interests are adverse to that person in a matter in which the information could be used to that person’s material disadvantage. “Confidential government information” means information obtained under governmental authority and which, at the time the rule is applied, the government is prohibited from disclosing to the public or has a legal privilege not to disclose and which is not otherwise available to the public.

rules.rule_statement: Except as expressly provided by law, a lawyer currently serving as a government employee is subject to the general ethics rules relating to current and former clients. Additionally, the government lawyer is not permitted to participate in a matter in which the lawyer participated personally and substantially while in private practice or nongovernmental employment, unless the appropriate government agency gives its informed consent, confirmed in writing. The California rule specifies “informed written consent.” California case law holds that when a conflicted private lawyer joins a public law office as its head supervising attorney, the entire office that he supervises must be vicariously disqualified.

General Conﬂict Rules Apply
Except as expressly provided by law, a lawyer currently serving as a government employee is subject to the general ethics rules relating to current and former clients. Additionally, the government lawyer is not permitted to participate in a matter in which the lawyer participated personally and substantially while in private practice or nongovernmental employment, unless the appropriate government agency gives its informed consent, confirmed in writing. The California rule specifies “informed written consent.” California case law holds that when a conflicted private lawyer joins a public law office as its head supervising attorney, the entire office that he supervises must be vicariously disqualified.

rules.rule_statement: A current government lawyer is not generally permitted to negotiate for private employment with any person who is involved as a party or lawyer for a party in a matter in which the lawyer is participating personally and substantially. A lawyer serving as a law clerk may negotiate for private employment, but only after the law clerk has notified the judge or other adjudicative officer for whom she works. To determine the existence of a conflict affecting a current or former government lawyer, the CA RPC define the term “matter” to include any judicial or other proceeding, application, request for a ruling or other determination, contract, transaction, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest, or other deliberation, decision, or action that is focused on the interests of specific persons, or a discrete and identifiable class of persons. The Model Rules include the above description but also include any other matter covered by the conflict-of- interest rules of the appropriate government agency.

No disclosure or consent is required if such nondisclosure or the compensation is otherwise authorized by law or court order or if the lawyer is rendering legal services on behalf of any public agency that provides legal services to other public agencies or to the public. EXAM NOTE: A common exam question involves a parent paying for representation of a son or daughter. Remember that a competent child, not the parent, is the client whose expressed interests govern.

rules.rule_statement: Rule A lawyer who represents an organization owes the duties of loyalty and confidentiality to the organization, not to its individual constituents.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule A lawyer who represents an organization owes the duties of loyalty and confidentiality to the organization, not to its individual constituents.
rules.source_text:
Organization As A Client
Rule A lawyer who represents an organization owes the duties of loyalty and confidentiality to the organization, not to its individual constituents.
topic_path: Influence By Persons Other Than Client

rules.canonical_name: Representing Both The Organization And An Employee
rules.rule_statement: A lawyer may represent both the organization (e.g., corporation) and its constituents (e.g., officers, directors, employees) as long as no other conflict exists. If the organization’s consent to the dual representation is required, then the consent must be given by an appropriate official within the organization other than the individual who is to be represented, or by the shareholders. In dealing with an organization’s constituents, a lawyer must explain the identity of the client for whom the lawyer acts, whenever it is or becomes apparent that the organization’s interests are or may become adverse to those of the constituents with whom the lawyer is dealing. The lawyer must not mislead such a constituent into believing that the constituent may communicate confidential information to the lawyer in a way that will not be used in the organization’s interest if that is or becomes adverse to the constituent.

Representing Both The Organization And An Employee
A lawyer may represent both the organization (e.g., corporation) and its constituents (e.g., officers, directors, employees) as long as no other conflict exists. If the organization’s consent to the dual representation is required, then the consent must be given by an appropriate official within the organization other than the individual who is to be represented, or by the shareholders. In dealing with an organization’s constituents, a lawyer must explain the identity of the client for whom the lawyer acts, whenever it is or becomes apparent that the organization’s interests are or may become adverse to those of the constituents with whom the lawyer is dealing. The lawyer must not mislead such a constituent into believing that the constituent may communicate confidential information to the lawyer in a way that will not be used in the organization’s interest if that is or becomes adverse to the constituent.

Upon learning of a constituent’s action (or intent to act or refusal to act) that the lawyer knows or reasonably should know is a violation of a legal obligation or a violation of the law that will likely cause substantial injury to the organization, the lawyer must proceed as is reasonably necessary in the best interest of the organization.
topic_path: Influence By Persons Other Than Client

rules.rule_statement: Unless the lawyer reasonably believes that it is not in the best interest of the organization to do so, the lawyer must refer the matter to higher authority in the organization, including, if warranted by the circumstances, the highest authority that can act on behalf of the organization as determined by applicable law. Under the CA RPC, the lawyer, in taking any action, must not reveal any privileged information.

Unless the lawyer reasonably believes that it is not in the best interest of the organization to do so, the lawyer must refer the matter to higher authority in the organization, including, if warranted by the circumstances, the highest authority that can act on behalf of the organization as determined by applicable law. Under the CA RPC, the lawyer, in taking any action, must not reveal any privileged information.

components.content: Forced to withdraw for reporting misconduct
components.source_text: 2)      Forced to withdraw for reporting misconduct

rules.rule_statement: If the lawyer has reported to the highest authority that can act on behalf of the organization and that authority insists on or fails to address in a timely and appropriate manner an action, or a refusal to act, that is a clear violation of law and the lawyer reasonably believes that the violation is reasonably certain to result in substantial injury to the organization, then the lawyer may reveal information relating to the representation, but only if and to the extent that the lawyer reasonably believes necessary to prevent the substantial injury. If the lawyer was engaged to investigate a violation of the law or defend the organization against a claim arising out of a violation of the law, then the lawyer is prohibited from revealing information related to her representation. ii)      California If the lawyer has reported to the highest authority that can act on behalf of the organization and that authority insists upon action, or fails to act, in a manner that is a violation of a legal obligation or a violation of the law that will likely cause substantial injury to the organization, the lawyer must proceed as is reasonably necessary in the best interests of the organization. The lawyer’s response may include the lawyer’s right and (when appropriate) duty to resign or withdraw. A lawyer who reasonably believes that she has been discharged because of reporting misconduct pursuant to the ethics rules, or who withdraws under circumstances that require or permit her to take action, must proceed as she reasonably believes necessary to ensure that the organization’s highest authority is informed of the lawyer’s discharge or withdrawal. rules.rule_statement: If one lawyer in a firm is unable to represent a client, then the prohibition applies to all other lawyers in the firm, unless it is based on a personal interest of one lawyer and does not present a significant risk of materially limiting the representation of the client by the firm. Such a disqualification may be waived by the affected client if: The lawyer reasonably believes that he will be able to provide competent and diligent representation to the affected client; ii. The representation is not prohibited by law; iii. The representation does not involve the assertion of a claim by one client against another client represented by the lawyer in the same litigation or other proceeding before a tribunal; and iv. The affected client gives informed consent, confirmed in writing (“informed written consent” under the CA RPC, which requires written disclosure of the potential adverse consequences of the client consenting to a conflicted representation). The rule of imputed disqualification does not prohibit representation by others in the firm when the person prohibited from involvement in a matter is a nonlawyer (e.g., paralegal or legal secretary). Such persons, however, must generally be screened from any personal participation in the matter to avoid communication of confidential information to others in the firm that both the nonlawyers and the firm have a legal duty to protect.

Exceptions

rules.rule_statement: Under the Model Rules, when a lawyer switches law firms, any conflict of interest that arises from the lawyer’s association with a prior law firm will not be imputed to the lawyer’s new firm if: The disqualified lawyer is timely screened from any participation in the matter and is apportioned no part of the fee; ii. Written notice is given to the affected former client to enable the former client to ascertain compliance with ethical rules; and iii. Certification of compliance with ethical rules and with screening procedures is provided to the former client by the screened lawyer and by a partner of the firm at reasonable intervals upon the former client’s written request and upon termination of the screening procedures.

Model Rules
Under the Model Rules, when a lawyer switches law firms, any conflict of interest that arises from the lawyer’s association with a prior law firm will not be imputed to the lawyer’s new firm if: The disqualified lawyer is timely screened from any participation in the matter and is apportioned no part of the fee; ii. Written notice is given to the affected former client to enable the former client to ascertain compliance with ethical rules; and iii. Certification of compliance with ethical rules and with screening procedures is provided to the former client by the screened lawyer and by a partner of the firm at reasonable intervals upon the former client’s written request and upon termination of the screening procedures.

rules.rule_statement: The California Rules are more restrictive and permit screening only in limited circumstances. Under the CA RPC, any conflict of interest that arises from the lawyer’s association with a prior law firm will not be imputed to the lawyer’s new firm if: iv. The prohibited lawyer did not substantially participate in the same or a substantially related matter; The disqualified lawyer is timely screened from any participation in the matter and is apportioned no part of the fee; and vi. Written notice is given to any affected former client to enable the former client to ascertain compliance with ethical rules. MR 1.10(a)(1); CA RPC Rule 1.10(a)(1). MR 1.7(b); CA RPC Rule 1.7(d). MR 1.10(a)(2). CA RPC Rule 1.10(a)(2).

rules.rule_statement: A lawyer must not represent anyone in a matter in which the lawyer participated personally and substantially as a judge or other adjudicative officer, judicial staff attorney, or law clerk to such a person, arbitrator, mediator, or other third-party neutral, unless all parties to the proceeding give informed written consent. A judge who was a member of a multimember court and then returned to private practice would not be prohibited from representing a client in a matter pending in the court but in which the former judge did not participate. Similarly, the fact that a former judge exercised administrative responsibility in a court would not prevent him from acting as a lawyer in a matter in which the judge previously exercised remote or incidental administrative responsibility that did not affect the merits of the case.
rules.short_rule_statement: A lawyer must not represent anyone in a matter in which the lawyer participated personally and substantially as a judge or other adjudicative officer, judicial staff attorney,...
rules.source_text:
General Rule
A lawyer must not represent anyone in a matter in which the lawyer participated personally and substantially as a judge or other adjudicative officer, judicial staff attorney, or law clerk to such a person, arbitrator, mediator, or other third-party neutral, unless all parties to the proceeding give informed written consent. A judge who was a member of a multimember court and then returned to private practice would not be prohibited from representing a client in a matter pending in the court but in which the former judge did not participate. Similarly, the fact that a former judge exercised administrative responsibility in a court would not prevent him from acting as a lawyer in a matter in which the judge previously exercised remote or incidental administrative responsibility that did not affect the merits of the case.

An arbitrator selected as a partisan of a party in a multimember arbitration panel is not prohibited from subsequently representing such a party.

rules.rule_statement: Under the Model Rules, if a lawyer is disqualified for participating personally and substantially as a judge or other adjudicative officer, law clerk, arbitrator, mediator or other third-party neutral, then no lawyer in a firm with which that lawyer is associated may knowingly undertake or continue representation in the matter unless: The disqualified lawyer is timely screened from any participation in the matter and gets no part of the fee; and ii. Written notice is promptly given to the parties and any appropriate tribunal to enable them to determine if the ethics rules have been met. The California Rule is the same but adds a requirement that the disqualification must not arise from the lawyer’s service as a mediator or settlement judge.

Disqualiﬁcation Of A Firm
Under the Model Rules, if a lawyer is disqualified for participating personally and substantially as a judge or other adjudicative officer, law clerk, arbitrator, mediator or other third-party neutral, then no lawyer in a firm with which that lawyer is associated may knowingly undertake or continue representation in the matter unless: The disqualified lawyer is timely screened from any participation in the matter and gets no part of the fee; and ii. Written notice is promptly given to the parties and any appropriate tribunal to enable them to determine if the ethics rules have been met. The California Rule is the same but adds a requirement that the disqualification must not arise from the lawyer’s service as a mediator or settlement judge.
topic_path: Conflicts Based On Lawyer'S Service As Arbitrator, Mediator, Or Judge

rules.rule_statement: Under the Model Rules, a lawyer participating personally and substantially as a judge or other adjudicative officer, arbitrator, mediator, or other third-party neutral may not negotiate for employment with any person who is either a party or lawyer for a party in the matter. A lawyer serving as a law clerk may negotiate for employment with a party or lawyer involved in a matter in which the clerk is participating personally and substantially only after the lawyer has notified the judge or other adjudicative officer for whom he works. The California Rule is generally the same, but also allows a lawyer serving as a judicial staff attorney, in addition to a lawyer serving as a law clerk, to negotiate for employment with the approval of the court.
rules.short_rule_statement: Under the Model Rules, a lawyer participating personally and substantially as a judge or other adjudicative officer, arbitrator, mediator, or other third-party neutral may not...
rules.source_text:
Negotiation For Employment
Under the Model Rules, a lawyer participating personally and substantially as a judge or other adjudicative officer, arbitrator, mediator, or other third-party neutral may not negotiate for employment with any person who is either a party or lawyer for a party in the matter. A lawyer serving as a law clerk may negotiate for employment with a party or lawyer involved in a matter in which the clerk is participating personally and substantially only after the lawyer has notified the judge or other adjudicative officer for whom he works. The California Rule is generally the same, but also allows a lawyer serving as a judicial staff attorney, in addition to a lawyer serving as a law clerk, to negotiate for employment with the approval of the court.

rules.canonical_name: Lawyer Serving As A Third-Party Neutral
rules.rule_statement: A lawyer serves as a “third-party neutral” when the lawyer assists two or more persons who are not clients of the lawyer to reach a resolution of a dispute or other matter that has arisen between them. Service as a third-party neutral may include service as an arbitrator, a mediator, or in such other capacity as will enable the lawyer to assist the parties to resolve the matter. A lawyer serving as a third-party neutral is required to inform unrepresented parties that the lawyer is not representing them. When the lawyer knows or reasonably should know that a party does not understand the lawyer’s role in the matter, the lawyer must explain the difference between the lawyer’s role as a third-party neutral and a lawyer’s role as one who represents a client. Service as a third-party neutral may include service as an arbitrator, a mediator, or in such other capacity as will enable the lawyer to assist the parties to resolve the matter. A lawyer serving as a third-party neutral is required to inform unrepresented parties that the lawyer is not representing them. When the lawyer knows or reasonably should know that a party does not understand the lawyer’s role in the matter, the lawyer must explain the difference between the lawyer’s role as a third-party neutral and a lawyer’s role as one who represents a client. rules.rule_statement: Ordinarily, a lawyer must not accept a representation that is adverse to a current or former client without obtaining informed written consent. However, a lawyer who participates in a short-term limited legal services program is subject to the conflict-of-interest rules only if she knows that the representation involves a conflict. Additionally, a lawyer has an imputed conflict of interest only if she knows that someone in her firm would have a conflict with the matter at hand. Finally, as a general matter, conflicts of interest arising from the lawyer’s representation in a short-term limited legal service program will not be imputed to her law firm. Short-term legal services programs are sponsored by courts, government agencies, bar associations, law schools, and nonprofit agencies and are designed for lawyers to assist individuals with their legal problems without the expectation that the representation will continue beyond the limited consultation. Examples of short-term limited legal services programs include legal-advice hotlines, advice-only clinics and pro se counseling programs. See MR 1.7; CA RPC Rule 1.7. MR 6.5(a)(1); CA RPC Rule 6.5(a)(1). MR 6.5(a)(2); CA RPC Rule 6.5(a)(2). MR 6.5(b); CA RPC Rule 6.5(b). See MR 6.5, cmt. 1; CA RPC Rule 6.5, cmt. 1.

Limited Legal Services Program
Ordinarily, a lawyer must not accept a representation that is adverse to a current or former client without obtaining informed written consent. However, a lawyer who participates in a short-term limited legal services program is subject to the conflict-of-interest rules only if she knows that the representation involves a conflict. Additionally, a lawyer has an imputed conflict of interest only if she knows that someone in her firm would have a conflict with the matter at hand. rules.rule_statement: Lawyers are encouraged to participate in and support legal services organizations. A lawyer may serve as a director, an officer, or a member of a legal services organization, apart from the law firm in which the lawyer practices, even if the organization serves persons having interests that are adverse to a client of the lawyer. Examples of legal services organizations include group legal service plans, referral services, legal aid providers, and public defender offices. The lawyer must not knowingly participate in a decision or action of the organization if such participation would be incompatible with the lawyer’s obligations to a client under the ethics rules concerning conflicts of interest, or when the decision or action could have a material adverse effect on the representation of a client of the organization whose interests are adverse to a client of the lawyer. MR 6.3; CA RPC Rule 6.3. MR 6.3; CA RPC Rule 6.3.

Examples of legal services organizations include group legal service plans, referral services, legal aid providers, and public defender offices. The lawyer must not knowingly participate in a decision or action of the organization if such participation would be incompatible with the lawyer’s obligations to a client under the ethics rules concerning conflicts of interest, or when the decision or action could have a material adverse effect on the representation of a client of the organization whose interests are adverse to a client of the lawyer. rules.rule_statement: Rule Under the Model Rules, a lawyer is obligated to provide competent representation to a client and must possess the legal knowledge, skill, thoroughness, and preparation reasonably necessary for the representation. The CA RPC require that a lawyer not intentionally, recklessly, with gross negligence, or repeatedly fail to perform legal services with competence and defines “competence” in any legal service as the application of the learning, skill, and mental, emotional, and physical ability reasonably necessary for the performance of such service.

Professional Competence
Rule Under the Model Rules, a lawyer is obligated to provide competent representation to a client and must possess the legal knowledge, skill, thoroughness, and preparation reasonably necessary for the representation. The CA RPC require that a lawyer not intentionally, recklessly, with gross negligence, or repeatedly fail to perform legal services with competence and defines “competence” in any legal service as the application of the learning, skill, and mental, emotional, and physical ability reasonably necessary for the performance of such service.

rules.rule_statement: Under the Model Rules, factors relevant to determining whether a lawyer has the requisite knowledge and skill include: The relative complexity and specialized nature of the matter; ii. The lawyer’s general experience; iii. The lawyer’s training and experience in the field in question; iv. The preparation and study the lawyer is able to give the matter; and Whether it is feasible to refer the matter to, or associate or consult with, a lawyer of established competence in the field in question. In some cases, all that is necessary is the proficiency of a general practitioner.

In General
Under the Model Rules, factors relevant to determining whether a lawyer has the requisite knowledge and skill include: The relative complexity and specialized nature of the matter; ii. The lawyer’s general experience; iii. The lawyer’s training and experience in the field in question; iv. The preparation and study the lawyer is able to give the matter; and Whether it is feasible to refer the matter to, or associate or consult with, a lawyer of established competence in the field in question. In some cases, all that is necessary is the proficiency of a general practitioner.

rules.rule_statement: A lawyer may accept representation if the requisite level of competence can be achieved by reasonable preparation. California requires sufficient learning and skill before performance. This also applies to a lawyer who is appointed as counsel for an unrepresented person. Competent representation can be provided through association with a lawyer of established competence in the field or referring the matter to another lawyer whom the lawyer reasonably believes to be competent.
rules.short_rule_statement: A lawyer may accept representation if the requisite level of competence can be achieved by reasonable preparation. California requires sufficient learning and skill before...
rules.source_text:
Competency Through Preparation Or Association
A lawyer may accept representation if the requisite level of competence can be achieved by reasonable preparation. California requires sufficient learning and skill before performance. This also applies to a lawyer who is appointed as counsel for an unrepresented person. Competent representation can be provided through association with a lawyer of established competence in the field or referring the matter to another lawyer whom the lawyer reasonably believes to be competent.

rules.rule_statement: In an emergency, a lawyer may give advice or assistance in a matter in which the lawyer does not have the skill ordinarily required if referral to or consultation or association with another lawyer would be impractical. However, assistance must be limited to what is reasonably necessary under the circumstances.

However, assistance must be limited to what is reasonably necessary under the circumstances.

rules.rule_statement: Under the Model Rules, whether a lawyer is properly prepared is determined by the degree of complexity and consequence of the matter. Major litigation and complex transactions ordinarily require more extensive treatment than matters of lesser complexity and consequence. A lawyer’s responsibility to prepare may further be limited by an agreement between the lawyer and client as to the scope of the representation.

rules.rule_statement: To maintain the requisite knowledge and skill, a lawyer should make efforts to keep abreast of changes in the law and its practice, including the benefits and risks associated with relevant technology, engage in continuing study and education, and comply with all continuing legal education requirements.

rules.rule_statement: A lawyer should not accept representation in a matter unless it can be performed competently. A lawyer who represents a client incompetently is subject to punishment by the disciplinary tribunal, regardless of causation or any financial loss to the client. A client cannot consent to the less-than-competent representation. Even if the client does consent (perhaps because the lawyer’s services are offered at a low fee or because of friendship), the lawyer providing incompetent services is subject to discipline. MR 1.1. rules.rule_statement: The lawyer must be dedicated and committed to the interests of the client despite obstruction or inconvenience to the lawyer. However, a lawyer is not bound to press for every possible advantage that might be realized for a client. The lawyer’s duty to act with reasonable diligence does not require the use of offensive tactics or prohibit treating all persons involved in the legal process with courtesy and respect.

rules.rule_statement: A lawyer must act with reasonable promptness in representing a client. A client’s interests can often be adversely affected by the passage of time or a change of conditions. Additionally, unreasonable delay can cause a client anxiety and undermine confidence in the trustworthiness of the lawyer. However, a lawyer’s duty to act with reasonable promptness does not prohibit the lawyer from agreeing to a reasonable request for a postponement that will not prejudice his client.

rules.rule_statement: The lawyer should follow through on all matters until completion as outlined by the agreement between the lawyer and the client. Whether the lawyer is obligated to prosecute an appeal for a client depends on the agreed-upon scope of the representation. If there is any doubt about the status of the relationship, the lawyer should clarify it, preferably in writing. Misleading a client about the status of the lawyer’s progress is its own violation separate from a diligence violation.

rules.rule_statement: Professional discipline is a penalty imposed on a lawyer by the state disciplinary authority for violating a rule of professional responsibility (i.e., the CA Rules). While the aggrieved client or anyone with knowledge of the violation initially brings a complaint to the state disciplinary authority, the state disciplinary authority prosecutes the violation. A grievance committee investigates the complaint, and if the committee finds merit to the charges, then it will hold a hearing limited to the charges brought in the complaint. The accused lawyer is entitled to notice, counsel, and an opportunity to be heard. He also has the right to introduce evidence and cross-examine witnesses. The burden of proof is on the party prosecuting the matter. If the committee finds that the charges are warranted, then it will recommend appropriate sanctions (e.g., private or public censure, suspension from practice, disbarment). The lawyer has the right to seek review of the committee’s decision by the highest court in the state.

rules.rule_statement: In addition to professional discipline, a lawyer who violates an ethics rule may potentially be subject to civil liability for malpractice. Sanctions for violation of the disciplinary rules are aimed at punishing a lawyer and protecting the public from future violations, but damages for malpractice are intended to compensate an injured client. A malpractice action is brought in a civil court and is prosecuted by the injured client. EXAM NOTE: Mere violation of a Model Rule will not automatically result in a ﬁnding of malpractice.  However, violation of a Model Rule is usually treated as evidence that the lawyer violated the duty of care.
rules.short_rule_statement: In addition to professional discipline, a lawyer who violates an ethics rule may potentially be subject to civil liability for malpractice. Sanctions for violation of the...

Malpractice
In addition to professional discipline, a lawyer who violates an ethics rule may potentially be subject to civil liability for malpractice. Sanctions for violation of the disciplinary rules are aimed at punishing a lawyer and protecting the public from future violations, but damages for malpractice are intended to compensate an injured client. A malpractice action is brought in a civil court and is prosecuted by the injured client. EXAM NOTE: Mere violation of a Model Rule will not automatically result in a ﬁnding of malpractice.  However, violation of a Model Rule is usually treated as evidence that the lawyer violated the duty of care.

rules.rule_statement: Malpractice can be based on a variety of legal theories, including the following. Breach of contract for A lawyer can be found liable for malpractice for breaching an express not fulfilling a duty or implied agreement with a client. Breach of the fiduciary A lawyer can be found liable for malpractice for breaching the relationship between the fiduciary duties of loyalty, confidentiality, and fair dealing. lawyer and client Intentional tort A lawyer can be liable for malpractice for intentional torts, including fraud, malicious prosecution, and abuse of process. Negligence Negligence is the most commonly asserted malpractice theory.

rules.rule_statement: In California, a lawyer who knows or reasonably should know that he does not have professional liability insurance must inform his client of that fact in writing at the beginning of the representation. Additionally, a lawyer must inform an existing client in writing that he no longer has professional liability insurance within 30 days of the termination of the insurance policy. Note that this rule does not apply to: A lawyer who knows or reasonably should know that the representation of the client in the matter will not exceed four hours; ii. A government lawyer or in-house counsel when the lawyer is representing or providing legal advice to a client in that capacity; iii. Legal services rendered in an emergency. MR 1.8(h)(1). CA RPC Rule 1.8.8(a). MR 1.8(h)(2); CA RPC Rule 1.8.8(b). Id. CA RPC Rule 1.4.2(b). CA RPC Rule 1.4.2(c).

rules.rule_statement: Model Rule 3.1 prohibits lawyers from bringing or defending a proceeding, or asserting or opposing an issue in a proceeding, unless there is a basis in law and fact for doing so that is not frivolous. A good-faith argument for an extension, modification, or reversal of existing law is not frivolous. “Frivolous” under the Model Rules means that the lawyer is unable either (1) to make a good-faith argument on the merits of the action taken or (2) to support the action taken by a good-faith argument for an extension, modification, or reversal of existing law. Filing an action, defense, or similar action taken for a client will not be frivolous merely because the facts have not first been fully substantiated or because the lawyer expects to develop critical evidence through the discovery process. An action is also not frivolous merely because the lawyer believes that the client’s position ultimately will not prevail. Under the Model Rules and the CA RPC, a lawyer for the defendant in a criminal proceeding, or the respondent in a proceeding that could result in incarceration, may nevertheless defend the proceeding as to require that every element of the case be established beyond a reasonable doubt, even if there is not a factual or legal basis for challenging the element.

rules.rule_statement: A lawyer must not present a claim or defense in litigation that is not warranted under existing law, unless it can be supported by a good-faith argument for an extension, modification, or reversal of an existing law. A lawyer is prohibited from counseling or maintaining an action, proceeding, or defense that does not appear legal or just, except for the defense of a person charged with a public offense. The SBA also forbids a lawyer from encouraging either the commencement or the continuance of an action or proceeding for any corrupt motive.

The standard for determining whether the lawyer conducted an objectively reasonable investigation into the factual and legal support for the claim or position is based on what a similarly situated lawyer would have done under the circumstances. Many states have a similar rule.

rules.rule_statement: Under the Model Rules, a lawyer must make reasonable efforts to expedite litigation consistent with the interests of the client. In doing so, a lawyer must balance the duty to use legal procedures to the fullest benefit of the client, with the duty not to abuse them. A lawyer will not be subject to discipline if a competent lawyer acting in good faith would regard the course of action taken as having some substantial purpose other than delay. Failure to expedite a matter for the purpose of frustrating an opposing party’s attempt to obtain rightful redress or repose is not reasonable. Realizing financial or other benefit from otherwise improper delay in litigation is not a legitimate interest of the client. Although a lawyer may sometimes properly seek a postponement for personal reasons, it is not proper for a lawyer to routinely fail to expedite litigation solely for the convenience of the advocates.

rules.canonical_name: Duty Of Candor To The Tribunal
rules.rule_statement: Under the Model Rules and the CA RPC, a lawyer representing a client in any proceedings of a tribunal has the duty of candor, discussed below in detail. This duty also applies when the lawyer is representing a client in an ancillary proceeding conducted under the tribunal’s adjudicative authority, such as a deposition. The duty of candor applies through the conclusion of the proceeding, which is defined as when a final judgment has been affirmed on appeal or the time for review has passed. It applies even if compliance requires disclosure of information otherwise protected by the ethical duty of confidentiality.

rules.rule_statement: Rule A lawyer must not knowingly make a false statement of law to the court. A lawyer is required to correct a false statement of law previously made to the tribunal by the lawyer. A lawyer must disclose controlling legal authority known to the lawyer to be directly adverse to the client’s position and not disclosed by opposing counsel. The lawyer may argue against the authority and seek to have it overturned or attempt to distinguish it from the case at bar. Under the CA RPC, a lawyer must not knowingly misquote the language of a book, statute, decision, or other authority.

A lawyer must disclose controlling legal authority known to the lawyer to be directly adverse to the client’s position and not disclosed by opposing counsel. The lawyer may argue against the authority and seek to have it overturned or attempt to distinguish it from the case at bar. Under the CA RPC, a lawyer must not knowingly misquote the language of a book, statute, decision, or other authority.
topic_path: Litigation And Other Forms Of Advocacy
Conduct In The Course Of Litigation

rules.rule_statement: Rule A lawyer must not knowingly make a false statement of fact to a tribunal or fail to correct a false statement of material fact previously made. Although a lawyer is responsible for pleadings and other documents prepared for litigation, she is not required to have personal knowledge of matters asserted in the pleadings and documents, which are generally assertions by the client, or by someone on the client’s behalf, and not assertions of the lawyer. Under the Model Rules, if an assertion purports to be based on the lawyer’s own knowledge (e.g., in an affidavit by the lawyer or in a statement made in open court), then it may be made only when the lawyer knows the assertion is true or believes it to be true following reasonably diligent inquiry. A lawyer is not under a general duty to reveal facts unfavorable to the client, unless doing so is necessary to avoid assisting the client in a criminal or fraudulent act. A lawyer who voluntarily offers adverse facts may be subject to discipline for breaching the duty of loyalty. However, under certain circumstance, a failure to disclose is the equivalent of an affirmative misrepresentation. Therefore, if the lawyer fails to correct a court’s erroneous understanding of a fact, that failure is improper.

rules.rule_statement: In an ex parte proceeding (e.g., an application for a temporary restraining order), a lawyer must inform the tribunal of all material facts known to the lawyer that will enable the tribunal to make an informed decision, regardless of whether the facts are adverse. Because there is no opposing counsel, there is no balance of presentation. The purpose of the tribunal is to obtain a just result, and it has an affirmative responsibility to accord the absent party just consideration. The lawyer for the represented party has the duty to disclose known material facts that the lawyer reasonably believes are necessary to an informed decision by the tribunal.

Because there is no opposing counsel, there is no balance of presentation. The purpose of the tribunal is to obtain a just result, and it has an affirmative responsibility to accord the absent party just consideration. The lawyer for the represented party has the duty to disclose known material facts that the lawyer reasonably believes are necessary to an informed decision by the tribunal.

rules.rule_statement: Rule A lawyer must not unlawfully obstruct another party’s access to evidence or unlawfully alter, destroy, or conceal a document or other material having potential evidentiary value. A lawyer must also not counsel or assist another person to do any such act. This rule applies to evidentiary material generally, including computerized information. The CA RPC specifically prohibit a lawyer from suppressing any evidence that the lawyer or the lawyer’s client has a legal obligation to reveal or produce.

A lawyer must also not counsel or assist another person to do any such act. This rule applies to evidentiary material generally, including computerized information. The CA RPC specifically prohibit a lawyer from suppressing any evidence that the lawyer or the lawyer’s client has a legal obligation to reveal or produce.

rules.rule_statement: A lawyer must not offer any inducement to a witness that is prohibited by law.

rules.rule_statement: If permitted by the law of the jurisdiction, a lawyer may properly pay a nonexpert witness the statutory fee and reasonable expenses (e.g., travel, lodging, meals, lost wages) incurred as a result of testifying.
rules.short_rule_statement: If permitted by the law of the jurisdiction, a lawyer may properly pay a nonexpert witness the statutory fee and reasonable expenses (e.g., travel, lodging, meals, lost wages)...
rules.source_text:
Nonexpert Witnesses
If permitted by the law of the jurisdiction, a lawyer may properly pay a nonexpert witness the statutory fee and reasonable expenses (e.g., travel, lodging, meals, lost wages) incurred as a result of testifying.
topic_path: Litigation And Other Forms Of Advocacy
Conduct In The Course Of Litigation

rules.rule_statement: An expert witness may be paid only the reasonable rate in the expert’s profession and compensated for reasonable expenses incurred as a result of testifying. Expert fees may not be contingent on the outcome of the matter. In addition to the general prohibition on offering inducements to witnesses under the Model Rules, the CA RPC specifically require that a lawyer not directly or indirectly pay, offer to pay, or acquiesce in the payment of compensation to a witness contingent upon the content of the witness’s testimony or the outcome of the case. Except when prohibited by law, a lawyer may advance, guarantee, or acquiesce in the payment of: Expenses reasonably incurred by a witness in attending or testifying; ii. Reasonable compensation to a witness for lost time in attending or testifying; or iii. A reasonable fee for the professional services of an expert witness. It is felony to bribe a witness to testify falsely or to refrain from testifying.

Expert Witnesses
An expert witness may be paid only the reasonable rate in the expert’s profession and compensated for reasonable expenses incurred as a result of testifying. Expert fees may not be contingent on the outcome of the matter. In addition to the general prohibition on offering inducements to witnesses under the Model Rules, the CA RPC specifically require that a lawyer not directly or indirectly pay, offer to pay, or acquiesce in the payment of compensation to a witness contingent upon the content of the witness’s testimony or the outcome of the case. Except when prohibited by law, a lawyer may advance, guarantee, or acquiesce in the payment of: Expenses reasonably incurred by a witness in attending or testifying; ii. Reasonable compensation to a witness for lost time in attending or testifying; or iii. A reasonable fee for the professional services of an expert witness. It is felony to bribe a witness to testify falsely or to refrain from testifying.

A lawyer is also prohibited from stating a personal opinion regarding:

rules.canonical_name: Prohibition On Falsifying Evidence Or Assisting In Witness Perjury
rules.rule_statement: Rule A lawyer must not falsify evidence and may not counsel a witness to testify falsely or assist a witness in testifying falsely. If a lawyer knows that a client intends to testify falsely or wants the lawyer to introduce false evidence, the lawyer should seek to persuade the client that the evidence should not be offered and, if unsuccessful, must refuse to offer the false evidence.

rules.rule_statement: Rule A lawyer is prohibited from knowingly offering false evidence and may refuse to offer evidence that she reasonably believes is false. The term “knows” means actual knowledge of the fact, which may be inferred from circumstances. EXAM NOTE: Examiners frequently test the subtle distinction between knowing and reasonably believing that evidence is false.  The prohibition against offering false evidence applies only if the lawyer knows that the evidence is false.  A lawyer’s reasonable belief that evidence is false does not preclude presentation of that evidence to the trier of fact.

Use Of False Evidence

The term “knows” means actual knowledge of the fact, which may be inferred from circumstances. EXAM NOTE: Examiners frequently test the subtle distinction between knowing and reasonably believing that evidence is false.  The prohibition against offering false evidence applies only if the lawyer knows that the evidence is false.  A lawyer’s reasonable belief that evidence is false does not preclude presentation of that evidence to the trier of fact.

rules.canonical_name: Learning Of False Evidence After It Has Been Offered
rules.rule_statement: If a lawyer, the lawyer’s client, or a witness called by the lawyer has offered material evidence and the lawyer comes to know of its falsity, the lawyer must take reasonable remedial measures. Reasonable remedial measures can include confidentially advising the client of the lawyer’s duty of candor and urging the client’s cooperation regarding the withdrawal or correction of the false statements or evidence. If that fails, the lawyer is required to take further action. If withdrawal from the representation is not permitted or will not undo the effect of the false evidence, then the lawyer must make such disclosure to the tribunal as is reasonably necessary to remedy the situation, even if doing so requires the lawyer to reveal information that otherwise would be protected as confidential by the Model Rules. In California, unlike under the Model Rules, remedial measures do not include disclosure of client confidential information that a lawyer is required to protect under CA RPC Rule 1.6 and SBA § 6068(e). Once the false evidence is disclosed, the tribunal will determine what should be done. It may make a statement about the matter to the trier of fact, order a mistrial, or do nothing upon finding that the matter is not material.

components.content: When the lawyer knows that testimony is or will be false
components.source_text: 1)      When the lawyer knows that testimony is or will be false

rules.rule_statement: Because of the special protections historically provided to criminal defendants, a lawyer must not refuse to offer the testimony of such a client when the lawyer reasonably believes but does not know that the testimony will be false. Unless the lawyer knows that the testimony will be false, the lawyer must honor the client’s decision to testify. A lawyer is required to take remedial measures if he knows that the testimony will be or was false. The lawyer must confidentially attempt to dissuade the client from testifying falsely or attempt to persuade the client to correct the false statement. Under the Model Rules, if the client refuses, then the lawyer must take further remedial measures. If the lawyer's compliance with the duty of candor results in such an extreme deterioration of the client-lawyer relationship that the lawyer can no longer competently represent the client, then the lawyer must seek permission to withdraw from representation. In connection with a request for permission to withdraw premised on a client’s misconduct, a lawyer may reveal information relating to the representation only to the extent reasonably necessary to comply with the Model Rules. If the tribunal denies withdrawal, then the lawyer must disclose the false testimony to the tribunal, even if doing so would reveal confidential information. California applies the narrative-statement approach to perjury by a criminal defendant. If a criminal defendant insists on testifying, and the lawyer knows that the testimony will be false, the lawyer may offer the testimony in a narrative form if the lawyer made reasonable efforts to dissuade the client from the unlawful conduct and the lawyer has sought permission from the court to withdraw, as required by the rules for terminating representation. A lawyer is under no duty to reveal false evidence or perjury that is discovered after the conclusion of the proceedings. A proceeding concludes when a final judgment in the proceeding has been affirmed on appeal or the time for review has passed. MR 3.3, cmt. 6; CA RPC Rule 3.3, cmt. 4. MR 3.3(a)(3); CA RPC Rule 3.3(a). MR 3.3(a)(3); CA RPC Rule 3.3(a)(3). CA RPC Rule 3.3(a)(3). MR 3.3(a)(3); CA RPC Rule 3.3(a)(3). MR 3.3, cmt. 15. See People v. Johnson, 62 Cal. App. 4th 608, 630 (1998). CA RPC Rule 3.3, cmt. 4. MR 3.3, cmt. 13; CA RPC Rule 3.3, cmt. 6.

rules.rule_statement: Rule Lawyers have a duty to communicate with clients, to keep them reasonably informed of the status of the matter, and to respond to their reasonable requests for information so that they can make informed decisions. A lawyer must promptly comply with reasonable requests for information and copies of significant documents when necessary to keep the client informed. A lawyer who receives an offer of settlement in a civil controversy or a proffered plea bargain in a criminal case must promptly inform the client of its substance unless the client previously indicated that the proposal will be acceptable or unacceptable or authorized the lawyer to accept or to reject the offer. California lawyers must keep the client reasonably informed about significant developments relating to the representation, such as the lawyer's receipt of funds on behalf of the client. A lawyer must promptly communicate to the client all terms and conditions of a proposed plea bargain or other dispositive offer made to the client in a criminal matter, and all amounts, terms, and conditions of any written offer of settlement made to the client in all other matters. An oral settlement offer made in a civil case must be communicated if it is a “significant development” under Rule 1.4.

rules.rule_statement: There are times when a lawyer may be justified in delaying transmission of information when the client would be likely to react imprudently. Example: A lawyer might withhold a client’s psychiatric diagnosis when the examining psychiatrist indicates that disclosing it would harm the client. Under the Model Rules, a lawyer may not withhold information to serve his or another’s interest or convenience. In some instances, local rules or court orders governing litigation may provide that information supplied to a lawyer may not be disclosed to the client. A lawyer is required to comply with those rules or orders. Under the California Rules, a lawyer may delay transmission of information to a client if the lawyer reasonably believes that the client would be likely to react in a way that may cause imminent harm to the client or others.

Withholding Information From A Client
There are times when a lawyer may be justified in delaying transmission of information when the client would be likely to react imprudently. Example: A lawyer might withhold a client’s psychiatric diagnosis when the examining psychiatrist indicates that disclosing it would harm the client. Under the Model Rules, a lawyer may not withhold information to serve his or another’s interest or convenience. In some instances, local rules or court orders governing litigation may provide that information supplied to a lawyer may not be disclosed to the client. A lawyer is required to comply with those rules or orders. Under the California Rules, a lawyer may delay transmission of information to a client if the lawyer reasonably believes that the client would be likely to react in a way that may cause imminent harm to the client or others.

Improper Ex Parte Communication

rules.rule_statement: The rule includes all forms of communication, including written communications. If a copy of a written communication is also sent to the other party or to opposing counsel, it will not be ex parte.

The rule includes all forms of communication, including written communications. If a copy of a written communication is also sent to the other party or to opposing counsel, it will not be ex parte.

rules.rule_statement: Only communications involving “housekeeping” matters (not involving the merits of the case) or emergency communications authorized by law or court order (e.g., emergency restraining orders) are permitted ex parte. EXAM NOTE: Look for questions placing a lawyer with good intentions in ex parte communication situations.  The lawyer is subject to discipline even though no harm is intended or results.

Permitted Communication
Only communications involving “housekeeping” matters (not involving the merits of the case) or emergency communications authorized by law or court order (e.g., emergency restraining orders) are permitted ex parte. EXAM NOTE: Look for questions placing a lawyer with good intentions in ex parte communication situations.  The lawyer is subject to discipline even though no harm is intended or results.

rules.rule_statement: Out-of-court communication with jurors or members of the panel from which jurors will be chosen is prohibited before and during the proceeding. This includes all communication, even about matters unrelated to the case. Lawyers are subject to discipline for failing to report misconduct related to jurors (including misconduct by the jurors themselves). Under both the Model Rules and the CA RPC, contact with a juror or prospective juror after she has been discharged is permitted unless: The jurisdiction prohibits such contact; ii. The juror expressed a desire not to talk with the lawyer; or iii. The communication involves misrepresentation, coercion, duress, or harassment. The CA RPC also prohibit communications intended to embarrass the juror or influence the juror’s actions in future jury service.

Communication With Jurors
Out-of-court communication with jurors or members of the panel from which jurors will be chosen is prohibited before and during the proceeding. This includes all communication, even about matters unrelated to the case. Lawyers are subject to discipline for failing to report misconduct related to jurors (including misconduct by the jurors themselves). Under both the Model Rules and the CA RPC, contact with a juror or prospective juror after she has been discharged is permitted unless: The jurisdiction prohibits such contact; ii. The juror expressed a desire not to talk with the lawyer; or iii. The communication involves misrepresentation, coercion, duress, or harassment. The CA RPC also prohibit communications intended to embarrass the juror or influence the juror’s actions in future jury service.

rules.canonical_name: No False Statements Of Material Fact Or Law
rules.rule_statement: In representing a client, a lawyer must not knowingly make a false statement of material fact or law to a third person. Although a lawyer does not generally have a duty to inform an opposing party of relevant facts, the lawyer is not permitted to misrepresent facts.

rules.source_text:
No False Statements Of Material Fact Or Law
In representing a client, a lawyer must not knowingly make a false statement of material fact or law to a third person. Although a lawyer does not generally have a duty to inform an opposing party of relevant facts, the lawyer is not permitted to misrepresent facts.
topic_path: Client Fraud And Perjury By A Client Or Witness
No False Statements Of Material Fact Or Law

rules.rule_statement: Misrepresentation can occur if the lawyer incorporates or affirms a statement of another person known to be false. Misrepresentations can also occur by partially true but misleading statements or omissions that are the equivalent of affirmative false statements. In addition to undergoing professional discipline, lawyers may be subject to charges of criminal or tortious misrepresentation.

rules.source_text:
Misrepresentation
Misrepresentation can occur if the lawyer incorporates or affirms a statement of another person known to be false. Misrepresentations can also occur by partially true but misleading statements or omissions that are the equivalent of affirmative false statements. In addition to undergoing professional discipline, lawyers may be subject to charges of criminal or tortious misrepresentation.

components.content: Failure to disclose a material fact when necessary to avoid assisting a client’s crime or fraud
components.label: d.
components.source_text: d.      Failure to disclose a material fact when necessary to avoid assisting a client’s crime or fraud

rules.rule_statement: Whether a statement is one of fact depends on the circumstances. In negotiations, certain types of statements are not generally considered statements of material fact (e.g., estimates of price on the subject of a transaction, a party’s intentions as to an acceptable settlement, the existence of an undisclosed principal, except when nondisclosure of the principal would constitute fraud). A lawyer must also disclose a material fact to a third person when disclosure is necessary to avoid assisting a client in the commission of a criminal or fraudulent act, unless disclosure is prohibited by the rules regarding confidentiality. Generally, a lawyer can avoid assisting a client’s commission of a crime or fraud by withdrawing from the representation. If the duty of confidentiality prohibits the lawyer from disclosing the facts and the lawyer’s continued representation would assist the client’s crime or fraud, then the lawyer is required to withdraw. The lawyer may notify the affected third person of the withdrawal. In some cases, it may be necessary for the lawyer to disaffirm an opinion, document, or affirmation he made. In extreme cases, the lawyer may be required to disclose information relating to the representation to avoid being deemed to have assisted in the crime or fraud. If the lawyer can avoid assisting a client’s crime or fraud only by disclosing this information, then the lawyer is required to do so, unless the disclosure is prohibited by the ethics rules.

rules.rule_statement: Rule A lawyer is not permitted to communicate about the subject of the representation with a person the lawyer knows is represented by another lawyer in the matter, unless the lawyer has the consent of the other lawyer or is authorized to do so by law or a court order.

Rule A lawyer is not permitted to communicate about the subject of the representation with a person the lawyer knows is represented by another lawyer in the matter, unless the lawyer has the consent of the other lawyer or is authorized to do so by law or a court order.
topic_path: Client Fraud And Perjury By A Client Or Witness
Communication With Persons Represented By Counsel

rules.rule_statement: This prohibition applies to communications with any person represented by counsel regarding the matter to which the communication relates. The rule applies even if the represented person initiates or consents to the communication. A lawyer must immediately terminate communication with a person if, after commencing communication, the lawyer learns that the person is represented by counsel in a matter to which the communication relates. The rule does not prohibit communication with a represented person, or an employee or agent of that person, regarding any matter outside the scope of the representation. The rule also does not prohibit communication with a represented person who is seeking advice from a lawyer who is not representing a client in the matter. A lawyer may not make a prohibited communication through the acts of another.
