rules.source_text:
Scope Of The Rule
This prohibition applies to communications with any person represented by counsel regarding the matter to which the communication relates. The rule applies even if the represented person initiates or consents to the communication. A lawyer must immediately terminate communication with a person if, after commencing communication, the lawyer learns that the person is represented by counsel in a matter to which the communication relates. The rule does not prohibit communication with a represented person, or an employee or agent of that person, regarding any matter outside the scope of the representation. The rule also does not prohibit communication with a represented person who is seeking advice from a lawyer who is not representing a client in the matter. A lawyer may not make a prohibited communication through the acts of another.

rules.rule_statement: Parties to a matter may communicate directly with each other. A lawyer is not prohibited from advising a client on communication that the client is legally entitled to make. Also, a lawyer having independent justification or legal authorization for communicating with a represented person is permitted to do so.

rules.canonical_name: Communications Authorized By Law Or Court Order
rules.rule_statement: Under the Model Rules, authorized communications may include communications by a lawyer on behalf of a client who is exercising a constitutional or other legal right to communicate with the government or investigative activities of lawyers representing governmental entities, directly or through investigative agents, before the commencement of criminal or civil enforcement proceedings. A lawyer who is uncertain whether a communication with a represented person is permissible may seek a court order.

rules.rule_statement: When, on behalf of a client, a lawyer deals with a person who is not represented by counsel, the lawyer is not permitted to give the impression that she is disinterested. When the lawyer knows or reasonably should know that the unrepresented person misunderstands the lawyer’s role, the lawyer must make reasonable efforts to correct the misunderstanding. The lawyer is not permitted to give legal advice to an unrepresented person, other than the advice to secure counsel, if the lawyer knows or reasonably should know that the interests of such a person are or have a reasonable possibility of being in conflict with the interests of the lawyer’s client.
rules.short_rule_statement: When, on behalf of a client, a lawyer deals with a person who is not represented by counsel, the lawyer is not permitted to give the impression that she is disinterested. When...
rules.source_text:
In General
When, on behalf of a client, a lawyer deals with a person who is not represented by counsel, the lawyer is not permitted to give the impression that she is disinterested. When the lawyer knows or reasonably should know that the unrepresented person misunderstands the lawyer’s role, the lawyer must make reasonable efforts to correct the misunderstanding. The lawyer is not permitted to give legal advice to an unrepresented person, other than the advice to secure counsel, if the lawyer knows or reasonably should know that the interests of such a person are or have a reasonable possibility of being in conflict with the interests of the lawyer’s client.

rules.rule_statement: The California rule does not include the “reasonable possibility of being in conflict” language, limiting this prohibition to interests that are actually in conflict. In California, a lawyer communicating with an unrepresented person on behalf of a client must not seek to obtain privileged or other confidential information the lawyer knows or reasonably should know the person may not reveal without violating a duty to another or which the lawyer is not otherwise entitled to receive.

rules.canonical_name: A Lawyer May Deal With An Unrepresented Person
rules.rule_statement: Neither the Model Rules nor the CA RPC prohibit a lawyer from negotiating the terms of a transaction or settling a dispute with an unrepresented person. As long as the lawyer has explained that she represents an adverse party and is not representing the person, the lawyer may inform the unrepresented person of the terms on which the lawyer’s client will enter into an agreement or settle a matter, prepare documents that require the person’s signature, and explain the lawyer’s own view of the meaning of the document or the lawyer’s view of the underlying legal obligations.

rules.rule_statement: Under the Model Rules, a lawyer who receives a document relating to the representation of the lawyer’s client and who knows or reasonably should know that the document was inadvertently sent must promptly notify the sender. This rule applies to all documents, including email or documents transmitted electronically that are subject to being read or put into readable form. Under the CA RPC, when it is reasonably apparent to a lawyer who receives a writing relating to a lawyer’s representation of a client that the writing was inadvertently sent or produced, and the lawyer knows or reasonably should know that the writing is privileged or subject to the work product doctrine, the lawyer must (1) refrain from examining the writing any more than is necessary to determine that it is privileged or subject to the work product doctrine, and (2) promptly notify the sender.

Receipt Of Inadvertently Sent Documents
Under the Model Rules, a lawyer who receives a document relating to the representation of the lawyer’s client and who knows or reasonably should know that the document was inadvertently sent must promptly notify the sender. This rule applies to all documents, including email or documents transmitted electronically that are subject to being read or put into readable form. Under the CA RPC, when it is reasonably apparent to a lawyer who receives a writing relating to a lawyer’s representation of a client that the writing was inadvertently sent or produced, and the lawyer knows or reasonably should know that the writing is privileged or subject to the work product doctrine, the lawyer must (1) refrain from examining the writing any more than is necessary to determine that it is privileged or subject to the work product doctrine, and (2) promptly notify the sender.

rules.rule_statement: A lawyer who is participating or has participated in the investigation or litigation of a matter is not permitted to make an extrajudicial statement that the lawyer knows or reasonably should know will be disseminated by public communication and will have a substantial likelihood of materially prejudicing an adjudicative proceeding in the matter.

rules.source_text:
General Rule
A lawyer who is participating or has participated in the investigation or litigation of a matter is not permitted to make an extrajudicial statement that the lawyer knows or reasonably should know will be disseminated by public communication and will have a substantial likelihood of materially prejudicing an adjudicative proceeding in the matter.
topic_path: Client Fraud And Perjury By A Client Or Witness

rules.rule_statement: A lawyer may make a statement that a reasonable lawyer would believe is required to protect a client from the substantial undue prejudicial effect of recent publicity that was not initiated by the lawyer or the client. The statement must be limited to information necessary to mitigate the recent adverse publicity.

rules.source_text:
Exception
A lawyer may make a statement that a reasonable lawyer would believe is required to protect a client from the substantial undue prejudicial effect of recent publicity that was not initiated by the lawyer or the client. The statement must be limited to information necessary to mitigate the recent adverse publicity.

rules.canonical_name: Facts About The Case May Be Discussed
rules.rule_statement: A lawyer participating in the investigation or litigation of a matter may state: The claim, offense, or defense involved and, except when prohibited by law, the identity of the persons involved; ii. Information that is contained in a public record; iii. That an investigation of a matter is in progress; iv. The scheduling or result of any step in litigation; A request for assistance in obtaining evidence and information necessary to the case; vi. A warning of danger concerning the behavior of a person involved, when there is reason to believe that the likelihood of substantial harm to an individual or to the public interest exists, but only to the extent that dissemination is reasonably necessary to protect the individual or the public; vii. The identity, residence, occupation and family status of a criminal defendant, information necessary to aid in apprehending an accused person in a criminal case, and the fact, time, and place of arrest; and viii. The identity of investigating and arresting officers or agencies and the length of the investigation. Example: Lawyer represents a famous actress in an action against a newspaper for libel. The case has attracted a lot of publicity, and a jury trial has been demanded. After one of the pretrial hearings, as Lawyer left the courthouse, a television news reporter asked him about the case on camera. In responding to the question, Lawyer truthfully stated, “The judge has upheld our right to subpoena the reporter involved, identified in our motion as Paparazzi, and question him on his mental impressions when he prepared his article.” Lawyer will not be subject to discipline for making the statement because it is a truthful statement relating to a matter of public record.

Facts About The Case May Be Discussed

rules.rule_statement: A key factor in determining prejudice can be the nature of the proceeding involved. A criminal jury trial will be most sensitive to extrajudicial speech, while a civil trial may be less sensitive. A nonjury hearing or arbitration proceedings may be even less sensitive. The Model Rules still place limitations on prejudicial comments in these cases, but the likelihood of prejudice differs depending on the type of proceeding. The Model Rules provide several examples of topics that are more likely than not to have a material prejudicial effect on a proceeding, particularly when they refer to a civil matter triable to a jury, a criminal matter, or any proceeding that could result in incarceration. These potentially prejudicial topics include: The character, credibility, reputation, or criminal record of a party, the suspect in a criminal investigation, or the witness; the identity of a witness; or the expected testimony of a party or witness; ii. The possibility of a guilty plea; the existence or contents of any confession, admission, or statement given by a defendant or suspect; or that person’s refusal or failure to make a statement; iii. The performance or results of any examination or test, the refusal or failure of a person to submit to an examination or test, or the identity or nature of physical evidence expected to be presented; iv. Any opinion as to the guilt or innocence of a defendant or suspect in a criminal case or proceeding that could result in incarceration; Information that the lawyer knows or reasonably should know is likely to be inadmissible as evidence in a trial and that would, if disclosed, create a substantial risk of prejudicing an impartial trial; and vi. The fact that a defendant has been charged with a crime, unless the remarks include a statement explaining that the charge is merely an accusation and that the defendant is presumed innocent until proven guilty. EXAM NOTE: Be certain to examine the facts of each case carefully.  Facts may or may not have a material prejudicial effect on a proceeding.

rules.rule_statement: The prosecutor is not only an advocate, but also a minister of justice. The prosecutor’s responsibilities include: The obligation to lay charges only when they are supported by probable cause; ii. To see that the defendant is given all of her constitutional due process rights, such as the right to counsel; iii. To seek a guilty verdict based on the evidence and a showing of proof beyond a reasonable doubt; and iv. To take special precautions to prevent and reverse the conviction of innocent defendants.

Special Responsibilities For The Prosecutor
The prosecutor is not only an advocate, but also a minister of justice. The prosecutor’s responsibilities include: The obligation to lay charges only when they are supported by probable cause; ii. To see that the defendant is given all of her constitutional due process rights, such as the right to counsel; iii. To seek a guilty verdict based on the evidence and a showing of proof beyond a reasonable doubt; and iv. To take special precautions to prevent and reverse the conviction of innocent defendants.

rules.canonical_name: Disclosure Of Evidence That May Help The Defendant
rules.rule_statement: A prosecutor is required to timely disclose all known exculpatory evidence or information that tends to disprove the guilt of the accused or mitigates the offense. Before sentencing, the prosecutor must disclose to the defense and the court all known, unprivileged mitigating information, except when a protective order of the court relieves the prosecutor of that duty.

rules.rule_statement: The prosecutor must disclose to the appropriate authority any new, credible, and material evidence creating a reasonable likelihood that a convicted defendant did not commit the offense of which he was convicted. If the conviction was obtained within the prosecutor’s jurisdiction, then the prosecutor must also: ii) Undertake further investigation to determine whether the defendant was wrongly convicted. The prosecutor must seek to reverse or set aside a conviction when the new exculpatory evidence is clear and convincing. As long as the prosecutor makes a good-faith, independent judgment that the evidence does not implicate the obligation to disclose, the prosecutor will not be subject to discipline.

The prosecutor must disclose to the appropriate authority any new, credible, and material evidence creating a reasonable likelihood that a convicted defendant did not commit the offense of which he was convicted. If the conviction was obtained within the prosecutor’s jurisdiction, then the prosecutor must also: ii) Undertake further investigation to determine whether the defendant was wrongly convicted. The prosecutor must seek to reverse or set aside a conviction when the new exculpatory evidence is clear and convincing. As long as the prosecutor makes a good-faith, independent judgment that the evidence does not implicate the obligation to disclose, the prosecutor will not be subject to discipline.

rules.rule_statement: Under the Model Rules, a prosecutor must not subpoena a lawyer to testify about past or current clients, except for statements that are necessary to inform the public of the nature and extent of the prosecutor’s action and that serve a legitimate law-enforcement purpose. Prosecutors must be objective when investigating leads without regard to whether the leads are favorable to the case. Prosecutors before a grand jury are required to present adverse material facts.
rules.short_rule_statement: Under the Model Rules, a prosecutor must not subpoena a lawyer to testify about past or current clients, except for statements that are necessary to inform the public of the...
rules.source_text:
Fairness
Under the Model Rules, a prosecutor must not subpoena a lawyer to testify about past or current clients, except for statements that are necessary to inform the public of the nature and extent of the prosecutor’s action and that serve a legitimate law-enforcement purpose. Prosecutors must be objective when investigating leads without regard to whether the leads are favorable to the case. Prosecutors before a grand jury are required to present adverse material facts.
topic_path: Client Fraud And Perjury By A Client Or Witness

rules.rule_statement: Except for statements that are necessary to inform the public of the nature and extent of the prosecutor's action and that serve a legitimate law enforcement purpose, prosecutors must refrain from making extrajudicial comments that have a substantial likelihood of heightening public condemnation of the accused and exercise reasonable care to prevent investigators, law enforcement personnel, employees, or other persons assisting or associated with the prosecutor in a criminal case from making an extrajudicial statement that the prosecutor would be prohibited from making.
rules.short_rule_statement: Except for statements that are necessary to inform the public of the nature and extent of the prosecutor's action and that serve a legitimate law enforcement purpose,...
rules.source_text:
Model Rules
Except for statements that are necessary to inform the public of the nature and extent of the prosecutor's action and that serve a legitimate law enforcement purpose, prosecutors must refrain from making extrajudicial comments that have a substantial likelihood of heightening public condemnation of the accused and exercise reasonable care to prevent investigators, law enforcement personnel, employees, or other persons assisting or associated with the prosecutor in a criminal case from making an extrajudicial statement that the prosecutor would be prohibited from making.
topic_path: Client Fraud And Perjury By A Client Or Witness

rules.rule_statement: A prosecutor in California must exercise reasonable care to prevent persons under the supervision or direction of the prosecutor, including investigators, law enforcement personnel, employees, or other persons assisting or associated with the prosecutor in a criminal case from making an extrajudicial statement that the prosecutor would be prohibited from making under the rules for trial publicity. This rule is meant to supplement CA RPC 3.6, which prohibits extrajudicial statements that have a substantial likelihood of prejudicing an adjudicatory proceeding. Because prosecutors must seek justice, not merely victory, they must disclose exculpatory evidence and mitigating circumstances regarding sentencing.

California
A prosecutor in California must exercise reasonable care to prevent persons under the supervision or direction of the prosecutor, including investigators, law enforcement personnel, employees, or other persons assisting or associated with the prosecutor in a criminal case from making an extrajudicial statement that the prosecutor would be prohibited from making under the rules for trial publicity. This rule is meant to supplement CA RPC 3.6, which prohibits extrajudicial statements that have a substantial likelihood of prejudicing an adjudicatory proceeding. Because prosecutors must seek justice, not merely victory, they must disclose exculpatory evidence and mitigating circumstances regarding sentencing.

rules.rule_statement: Advertising refers to widely distributed public statements (e.g., phone book listings, newspaper or television ads) about the services available from a lawyer or law firm. Solicitation refers to narrower, targeted communications directed at a small group identified as requiring a specific service (e.g., letters sent to accident victims, in-person solicitation of employment, real-time electronic contact). Lawyers who advertise or solicit prospective clients are subject to restrictions on those activities. See MR 7.1–7.3; CA RPC Rules 7.1-7.3; and SBA §§ 6150–6159.2.
rules.short_rule_statement: Advertising refers to widely distributed public statements (e.g., phone book listings, newspaper or television ads) about the services available from a lawyer or law firm....
rules.source_text:
Advertising And Solicitation
Advertising refers to widely distributed public statements (e.g., phone book listings, newspaper or television ads) about the services available from a lawyer or law firm. Solicitation refers to narrower, targeted communications directed at a small group identified as requiring a specific service (e.g., letters sent to accident victims, in-person solicitation of employment, real-time electronic contact). Lawyers who advertise or solicit prospective clients are subject to restrictions on those activities. See MR 7.1–7.3; CA RPC Rules 7.1-7.3; and SBA §§ 6150–6159.2.

rules.rule_statement: Rule A lawyer must not make false or misleading statements about the lawyer or her services. A communication is false or misleading if it contains a material representation of law or fact or omits a fact necessary to make the communication as a whole not materially misleading.

rules.source_text:
Advertising
Rule A lawyer must not make false or misleading statements about the lawyer or her services. A communication is false or misleading if it contains a material representation of law or fact or omits a fact necessary to make the communication as a whole not materially misleading.

rules.rule_statement: Under the CA RPC, the following communications are considered false or misleading: Communications that contain an express guarantee or warranty of the result of a particular representation; ii. Communications that truthfully report a lawyer’s achievements on behalf of clients or former clients, or a testimonial about or endorsement of the lawyer, if presented so as to lead a reasonable person to form an unjustified expectation that the same results could be obtained for other clients in similar matters without reference to the specific factual and legal circumstances of each client’s case; iii. Communications that state or imply “no fee without recovery” unless those communications also expressly disclose whether the client will be liable for costs; and iv) Communications that state or imply that a lawyer can provide legal services in a language other than English unless the member can actually provide legal services in such language, or the communication also states in the language of the communication the employment title of the person who speaks such language.

rules.canonical_name: Advertiser Must Be Identiﬁed In The Communication
rules.rule_statement: Under the CA RPC, any advertisement must include the name and address of at least one lawyer or firm responsible for its content. Under the Model Rules, instead of the “address,” the advertisement must include the “contact information” (e.g., a website address, a telephone number, an email address, or a physical office location) of at least one lawyer or firm responsible for its content.

rules.rule_statement: Rule A lawyer may communicate the fact that the lawyer does or does not practice in particular fields of law. A lawyer generally may state that he is a “specialist,” practices a “specialty,” or “specializes in” particular fields, but he must not violate Rule 7.1 prohibiting false or misleading communications. A lawyer may not state or imply that she is certified as a specialist in a particular field of law, unless: accredited by the ABA; and ii) The name of the certifying organization is clearly identified in the communication. States may not impose blanket prohibitions on statements of certification. A lawyer admitted to practice before the United States Patent and Trademark Office may use the designation “Patent Attorney” or a substantially similar designation. A lawyer engaged in admiralty practice may use the designation “Admiralty,” “Proctor in Admiralty,” or a substantially similar designation.

Statements About Fields Of Practice
Rule A lawyer may communicate the fact that the lawyer does or does not practice in particular fields of law. A lawyer generally may state that he is a “specialist,” practices a “specialty,” or “specializes in” particular fields, but he must not violate Rule 7.1 prohibiting false or misleading communications. A lawyer may not state or imply that she is certified as a specialist in a particular field of law, unless: accredited by the ABA; and ii) The name of the certifying organization is clearly identified in the communication. States may not impose blanket prohibitions on statements of certification. A lawyer admitted to practice before the United States Patent and Trademark Office may use the designation “Patent Attorney” or a substantially similar designation. A lawyer engaged in admiralty practice may use the designation “Admiralty,” “Proctor in Admiralty,” or a substantially similar designation.

rules.rule_statement: ii) Statements that the featured lawyer can obtain immediate cash or quick settlements; iii) Impersonations or electronic images of any person other than the lawyer purporting to be a lawyer; iv) Impersonations or electronic images of any person purporting to be a client of the featured lawyer, or dramatizations of events, unless fully disclosed as such in the advertisement; function; and vi) Any statement offering representation on a contingent basis unless it also indicates whether a client will be held responsible for any costs advanced by the lawyer when no recovery is obtained. If the client will not be held responsible for costs, then no disclosure is required. A California lawyer may not claim to be a “certified specialist” unless he holds a current certificate as a specialist issued by the Board of Legal Specialization or other entity accredited by the State Bar of California to designate specialists under standards adopted by the Board of Trustees. rules.rule_statement: Rule A lawyer may not use a firm name, trade name, or other professional designation that is misleading. A trade name may be used by a lawyer in private practice if it does not imply a connection with a government agency or with a public or charitable legal services organization and is not otherwise in violation of the ethics rules. Under the Model Rules, the name of a lawyer holding a public office must not be used in the name of a law firm, or in communications on its behalf, during any substantial period in which the lawyer is not actively and regularly practicing with the firm. Additionally, a law firm with offices in more than one jurisdiction may use the same name in each jurisdiction. MR 7.1; CA RPC Rule 7.1. CA RPC Rule 7.1 CA RPC Rule 7.2(c). Firm Names And Trade Names

rules.rule_statement: The Model Rules defines solicitation as a communication initiated by or on behalf of a lawyer or law firm that is directed to a specific person the lawyer knows or reasonably should know needs legal services in a particular matter and that offers to provide, or reasonably can be understood as offering to provide, legal services for that matter. The CA RPC defines solicitation as a targeted communication offered by or on behalf of a lawyer that is directed to a specific person and that offers to provide, or can reasonably be understood as offering to provide, legal services. A lawyer’s communication is not a solicitation if it is directed to the general public (e.g., through a billboard, internet banner advertisement, website, or a television commercial).
rules.short_rule_statement: The Model Rules defines solicitation as a communication initiated by or on behalf of a lawyer or law firm that is directed to a specific person the lawyer knows or reasonably...
rules.source_text:
Solicitation
The Model Rules defines solicitation as a communication initiated by or on behalf of a lawyer or law firm that is directed to a specific person the lawyer knows or reasonably should know needs legal services in a particular matter and that offers to provide, or reasonably can be understood as offering to provide, legal services for that matter. The CA RPC defines solicitation as a targeted communication offered by or on behalf of a lawyer that is directed to a specific person and that offers to provide, or can reasonably be understood as offering to provide, legal services. A lawyer’s communication is not a solicitation if it is directed to the general public (e.g., through a billboard, internet banner advertisement, website, or a television commercial).

rules.rule_statement: Under the CA RPC, solicitation of prospective clients in person, by live telephone, or by real-time electronic contact is prohibited when a significant motive for the lawyer’s action is the lawyer’s pecuniary gain, unless the person contacted is a lawyer or has a family, close personal, or prior professional relationship with the soliciting lawyer. By contrast, the Model Rules do not prohibit real-time electronic contact (e.g., text messages, chat rooms, or other written communications that recipients may easily disregard). Under the Model Rules, solicitation of professional employment by live person- to-person contact is prohibited when a significant motive for the lawyer’s doing so is the lawyer’s or law firm’s pecuniary gain, unless the person contacted is: A lawyer; ii. A person who has a family, close personal, or prior business or professional relationship with the soliciting lawyer or law firm; or iii. A person who routinely uses for business purposes the type of legal services offered by the lawyer. “Live person-to-person contact” means in-person, face-to-face, live telephone, and other real-time visual or auditory person- to-person communications in which the person is subject to a direct personal encounter without time for reflection.

rules.rule_statement: Prospective clients, overwhelmed by the circumstances giving rise to the need for legal services, may find it difficult to evaluate alternatives with reasoned judgment in the face of the lawyer’s insistence upon an immediate response. Because this situation raises strong possibilities of undue influence, intimidation, and overreaching, it is prohibited.

Prospective clients, overwhelmed by the circumstances giving rise to the need for legal services, may find it difficult to evaluate alternatives with reasoned judgment in the face of the lawyer’s insistence upon an immediate response. Because this situation raises strong possibilities of undue influence, intimidation, and overreaching, it is prohibited.

rules.canonical_name: Allowed If Not For Pecuniary Gain
rules.rule_statement: Prohibition of solicitation applies if a significant motive of the lawyer’s solicitation of the client is for the lawyer’s pecuniary gain. A lawyer who offers to perform on a pro bono basis may engage in solicitation.

rules.rule_statement: Rule Lawyers who employ agents to monitor accidents and other events likely to produce legal work and to solicit business for the lawyer are subject to discipline. In California, use of runners or cappers is a criminal violation. Any contract for runners and cappers is specifically void as illegal in California. Use of a state bar–authorized lawyer-referral service, legal insurance, prepaid legal service plan, or pro bono network is not subject to this prohibition.
rules.short_rule_statement: Rule Lawyers who employ agents to monitor accidents and other events likely to produce legal work and to solicit business for the lawyer are subject to discipline. In...
rules.source_text:
Runners And Cappers
Rule Lawyers who employ agents to monitor accidents and other events likely to produce legal work and to solicit business for the lawyer are subject to discipline. In California, use of runners or cappers is a criminal violation. Any contract for runners and cappers is specifically void as illegal in California. Use of a state bar–authorized lawyer-referral service, legal insurance, prepaid legal service plan, or pro bono network is not subject to this prohibition.

rules.canonical_name: Duress, Coercion, Or Harassment Of Prospective Clients
rules.rule_statement: A lawyer is not permitted to solicit employment from a prospective client if: iv. The prospective client has communicated the desire not to be solicited by the lawyer; or The solicitation involves coercion, duress, or harassment.

rules.rule_statement: Unless a prospective client has expressed a desire not to be solicited by the lawyer, a lawyer may issue written, recorded, or electronic communications (such as email) that satisfy the appropriate ethics rules regarding advertising and communications.

Targeted Mailings, Recordings, Or Advertisements
Unless a prospective client has expressed a desire not to be solicited by the lawyer, a lawyer may issue written, recorded, or electronic communications (such as email) that satisfy the appropriate ethics rules regarding advertising and communications.

rules.canonical_name: California Requirement To Label The Targeted Solicitation As Advertising
rules.rule_statement: Under the CA RPC, all written, recorded, or electronic communication from a lawyer soliciting professional employment from any person known to be in need of legal services in a particular matter must include the word “Advertisement” or words of similar import (such as “advertising material”) on the outside of mailing envelopes, if any, and at the beginning and ending of all recorded or electronic communications. The Model Rules no longer have a similar requirement.

The Model Rules no longer have a similar requirement.

rules.rule_statement: Some states impose mandatory waiting periods before attorney solicitation of accident victims and their families can occur. Narrowly drawn waiting periods are constitutional. Federal law (49 U.S.C. § 1136(g)(2)) prohibits lawyers from contacting victims of airplane accidents or their families until at least 45 days from the date of the accident.

Post-Event Waiting Periods
Some states impose mandatory waiting periods before attorney solicitation of accident victims and their families can occur. Narrowly drawn waiting periods are constitutional. Federal law (49 U.S.C. § 1136(g)(2)) prohibits lawyers from contacting victims of airplane accidents or their families until at least 45 days from the date of the accident.

rules.canonical_name: Prepaid Or Group Legal Services Plan

Went For It, Inc., 515 U.S. 618, 623-24 (1995). MR 7.3(e); CA RPC Rule 7.3(d).

Prepaid Or Group Legal Services Plan
A lawyer may participate with a prepaid or group legal service plan operated by an organization not owned or directed by the lawyer that uses in?person or telephone contact to solicit memberships or subscriptions for the plan from persons who are not known to need legal services in a particular matter covered by the plan. CA RPC Rule 7.3(e). MR 7.3, cmt. 1; CA RPC Rule 7.3, cmt. 1. CA RPC Rule 7.3(a). MR 7.3(b). MR 7.3, cmt. 2. See MR 5.3(c). MR 7.3(c); CA RPC Rule 7.3(b). CA RPC Rule 7.3(c). Fla. Bar v. Went For It, Inc., 515 U.S. 618, 623-24 (1995). MR 7.3(e); CA RPC Rule 7.3(d).

rules.canonical_name: Maintaining The Integrity Of The Profession
rules.rule_statement: A license is a prerequisite to the practice of law. The state has an interest in maintaining the integrity of the legal profession to protect the public from incompetent or unscrupulous practitioners.

rules.rule_statement: The ABA is a voluntary association with no direct regulatory authority. States license lawyers to practice law within the state. Membership in a state bar association is voluntary in some states and mandatory in others.

The ABA is a voluntary association with no direct regulatory authority. States license lawyers to practice law within the state. Membership in a state bar association is voluntary in some states and mandatory in others.

rules.rule_statement: States impose educational, knowledge, and character requirements on applicants for admission to the practice of law. A lawyer must not assist in the admission of an unqualified applicant.

Requirements To Practice
States impose educational, knowledge, and character requirements on applicants for admission to the practice of law. A lawyer must not assist in the admission of an unqualified applicant.

rules.rule_statement: Most states require graduation from an ABA-accredited law school for admission to practice. A few states (including California) require only a state accreditation.

Granting a right to appear pro hac vice is within the discretion of the jurisdiction and the presiding judge.

Such admission is limited, and it generally requires supervision by a California lawyer and registration with the State Bar of California.

rules.rule_statement: An applicant for admission to the bar is prohibited from: Knowingly (or with a reckless disregard as to its truth or falsity under the CA RPC) making a false statement of material fact in connection with the application; ii. Failing to disclose a fact necessary to correct a misapprehension known to have arisen in the matter; or iii. Failing to respond to a lawful demand by an admissions authority in connection with the application (unless such fact is protected by the rules governing confidential communications).

rules.rule_statement: The State Bar of California has the power to regulate all aspects of the practice of law. California is unique in having its own independent, full-time judiciary to specifically rule on attorney discipline matters in the State Bar Court. The procedure begins with an investigation by the State Bar of California, followed by referral to the State Bar Court by the Office of Chief Trial Counsel. Following a hearing, the State Bar Court forwards a recommendation to the California Supreme Court. The State Bar of California’s disciplinary authority stems from Rule 8.1.1 of the CA RPC, SBA Article 5, and Rule 9.10 of the California Rules of Court.
rules.short_rule_statement: The State Bar of California has the power to regulate all aspects of the practice of law. California is unique in having its own independent, full-time judiciary to...

California State Bar
The State Bar of California has the power to regulate all aspects of the practice of law. California is unique in having its own independent, full-time judiciary to specifically rule on attorney discipline matters in the State Bar Court. The procedure begins with an investigation by the State Bar of California, followed by referral to the State Bar Court by the Office of Chief Trial Counsel. Following a hearing, the State Bar Court forwards a recommendation to the California Supreme Court. The State Bar of California’s disciplinary authority stems from Rule 8.1.1 of the CA RPC, SBA Article 5, and Rule 9.10 of the California Rules of Court.

rules.rule_statement: It is professional misconduct for a lawyer to: Violate or attempt to violate any of the ethics rules (or the SBA in California), knowingly assist or induce another to do so, or to do so through the acts of another; ii. Commit a criminal act that reflects adversely on the lawyer’s honesty, trustworthiness, or fitness as a lawyer in other respects; iii. Engage in conduct involving dishonesty, fraud, deceit, or misrepresentation (the conduct need not be criminal); iv. Engage in conduct prejudicial to the administration of justice (the conduct need not be criminal or dishonest); State or imply an ability to influence improperly a government agency or official; or vi. Knowingly assist a judge or judicial officer in conduct that is a violation of applicable rules of judicial conduct or other law. The lawyer does not have to be engaged in the practice of law to violate the ethics rules and be subject to professional discipline.
rules.short_rule_statement: It is professional misconduct for a lawyer to: Violate or attempt to violate any of the ethics rules (or the SBA in California), knowingly assist or induce another to do so, or...
rules.source_text:
Grounds Of Misconduct
It is professional misconduct for a lawyer to: Violate or attempt to violate any of the ethics rules (or the SBA in California), knowingly assist or induce another to do so, or to do so through the acts of another; ii. Commit a criminal act that reflects adversely on the lawyer’s honesty, trustworthiness, or fitness as a lawyer in other respects; iii. Engage in conduct involving dishonesty, fraud, deceit, or misrepresentation (the conduct need not be criminal); iv. Engage in conduct prejudicial to the administration of justice (the conduct need not be criminal or dishonest); State or imply an ability to influence improperly a government agency or official; or vi. Knowingly assist a judge or judicial officer in conduct that is a violation of applicable rules of judicial conduct or other law. The lawyer does not have to be engaged in the practice of law to violate the ethics rules and be subject to professional discipline.
topic_path: Maintaining The Integrity Of The Profession

rules.rule_statement: A lawyer is subject to discipline for misconduct committed by another if the lawyer: Orders the misconduct; ii. Ratifies the misconduct with knowledge of the specific conduct; or iii. As a partner in a law firm, a lawyer with managerial authority, or a lawyer with direct supervisory authority over another lawyer or law-related employee, knows of the conduct at a time when its consequences can be avoided or mitigated but fails to take reasonable remedial action.
rules.short_rule_statement: A lawyer is subject to discipline for misconduct committed by another if the lawyer: Orders the misconduct; ii. Ratifies the misconduct with knowledge of the specific conduct;...
rules.source_text:
In General
A lawyer is subject to discipline for misconduct committed by another if the lawyer: Orders the misconduct; ii. Ratifies the misconduct with knowledge of the specific conduct; or iii. As a partner in a law firm, a lawyer with managerial authority, or a lawyer with direct supervisory authority over another lawyer or law-related employee, knows of the conduct at a time when its consequences can be avoided or mitigated but fails to take reasonable remedial action.
topic_path: Maintaining The Integrity Of The Profession

rules.rule_statement: A partner is a member of a partnership, a shareholder in a law firm organized as a professional corporation, or a member of an association authorized to practice law. Partners in a law firm and other lawyers with managerial authority must make reasonable efforts to ensure that the firm has measures in place that give reasonable assurance that all lawyers in the firm conform to the ethics rules and that the conduct of all nonlawyers working for the firm conforms to the ethics rules. The definition of reasonable measures depends on the size and kind of firm. Partners may be subject to discipline for ratifying conduct by a nonlawyer if the conduct would have violated the disciplinary rules if it had been engaged in by a lawyer. EXAM NOTE: The obligations of partners are tested frequently in the context of paralegals or nonlawyers making mistakes without the lawyer’s knowledge.  The outcome depends on whether the ﬁrm’s procedures were established and followed.

Partners In A Law Firm

The definition of reasonable measures depends on the size and kind of firm. Partners may be subject to discipline for ratifying conduct by a nonlawyer if the conduct would have violated the disciplinary rules if it had been engaged in by a lawyer. EXAM NOTE: The obligations of partners are tested frequently in the context of paralegals or nonlawyers making mistakes without the lawyer’s knowledge.  The outcome depends on whether the ﬁrm’s procedures were established and followed.

rules.rule_statement: A lawyer with direct supervisory authority over another lawyer or nonlawyer must make reasonable efforts to ensure that the supervised person’s conduct conforms to the ethics rules (and the SBA in California). EXAM NOTE: Supervisory obligations are frequently tested in situations in which nonlawyers are not properly instructed of the need to maintain conﬁdential communications.

Direct Supervisor
A lawyer with direct supervisory authority over another lawyer or nonlawyer must make reasonable efforts to ensure that the supervised person’s conduct conforms to the ethics rules (and the SBA in California). EXAM NOTE: Supervisory obligations are frequently tested in situations in which nonlawyers are not properly instructed of the need to maintain conﬁdential communications.
topic_path: Maintaining The Integrity Of The Profession

rules.rule_statement: A subordinate lawyer must conform to the ethics rules even if acting under the direction of a supervising lawyer. However, the subordinate lawyer does not violate the ethics rule, if he acts in accordance with the supervising lawyer’s reasonable resolution of an arguable question of professional duty. Note that if an ethical issue can be reasonably answered in only one specific way, it will not constitute an arguable question. Note also that to know whether a supervising lawyer’s resolution is in fact reasonable, the subordinate lawyer has a responsibility to undertake his own analysis of the law applicable to the supervising lawyer’s conduct. Example: Supervisor and Associate disagree regarding their responsibility to respond to a request for production of documents. The request is ambiguous, and after careful consideration of the rules, Associate follows Supervisor’s direction not to produce certain documents. A court subsequently finds that the refusal to produce the documents constituted a violation of the Model Rules. Under the Model Rules and the CA RPC, Supervisor will be held to have violated the rule, but Associate will not.

rules.canonical_name: Misconduct Outside Of The State Or Jurisdiction
rules.rule_statement: A lawyer will be subject to discipline for misconduct in any state or jurisdiction where the lawyer is admitted to practice, even if the misconduct occurred outside of that state or jurisdiction.
rules.short_rule_statement: A lawyer will be subject to discipline for misconduct in any state or jurisdiction where the lawyer is admitted to practice, even if the misconduct occurred outside of that...
rules.source_text:
Misconduct Outside Of The State Or Jurisdiction
A lawyer will be subject to discipline for misconduct in any state or jurisdiction where the lawyer is admitted to practice, even if the misconduct occurred outside of that state or jurisdiction.
topic_path: Maintaining The Integrity Of The Profession
Misconduct Outside Of The State Or Jurisdiction

rules.rule_statement: Rule A lawyer is required to report misconduct by another lawyer to the appropriate professional authority (e.g., disciplinary agency) when the lawyer has actual knowledge of the misconduct. The duty to report is limited to “material matters of clear and weighty importance” that raise a substantial question regarding the lawyer’s honesty or professional fitness. When the lawyer learns of misconduct through a communication protected by the duty of confidentiality, the lawyer is not required to report it, unless it falls under the future-crime exception. Actual knowledge may be inferred from the circumstances and includes learning of the misconduct from a reliable source with independent corroboration. A lawyer who fails to report misconduct by another lawyer will be subject to discipline.

Model Rules
Rule A lawyer is required to report misconduct by another lawyer to the appropriate professional authority (e.g., disciplinary agency) when the lawyer has actual knowledge of the misconduct. The duty to report is limited to “material matters of clear and weighty importance” that raise a substantial question regarding the lawyer’s honesty or professional fitness. When the lawyer learns of misconduct through a communication protected by the duty of confidentiality, the lawyer is not required to report it, unless it falls under the future-crime exception. Actual knowledge may be inferred from the circumstances and includes learning of the misconduct from a reliable source with independent corroboration. A lawyer who fails to report misconduct by another lawyer will be subject to discipline.

rules.rule_statement: Rule The CA RPC require a lawyer, without undue delay, to inform the State Bar of California or an appropriate tribunal when the lawyer knows of credible evidence that another lawyer engaged in misconduct. The lawyer may, but is not required to, report other violations of the CA RPC or the State Bar Act to the State Bar of California. By statute, California also requires a lawyer to report himself to the State Bar of California in writing within 30 days of the time that the lawyer has knowledge of any of the following: iv. Filing of three or more lawsuits in a 12-month period against the lawyer for malpractice or other wrongful conduct committed in a professional capacity; Entry of judgment against the lawyer in a civil action for fraud, misrepresentation, breach of fiduciary duty, or gross negligence committed in a professional capacity; vi. Imposition of judicial sanctions against the lawyer, except for sanctions for failure to make discovery or monetary sanctions of less than $1,000; vii. Bringing an indictment or information charging a felony against the lawyer; viii. Conviction of the lawyer on a felony, or a misdemeanor committed in the course of the practice of law, or in a manner in which a client of the lawyer was the victim, or a necessary element of which involves improper conduct of a lawyer, including dishonesty or other moral turpitude, or an attempt or a conspiracy, or solicitation of another to commit a felony or a misdemeanor of that type; ix. Imposition of discipline against the lawyer by a professional or occupational disciplinary agency or licensing board, whether in California or elsewhere; or Reversal of judgment in a proceeding based in whole or in part upon willful misrepresentation, grossly incompetent representation, or misconduct by a lawyer. Failure to report such misconduct may serve as a basis for discipline.

rules.rule_statement: The Model Rules do not set forth particular sanctions for lawyer misconduct. In California, the sanctions applicable for misconduct include: vii. Permanent disbarment; viii. Suspension; ix. Public and private censure; and Reprimand. The burden of proving misconduct rests with the State Bar Court and, ultimately, with the California Supreme Court to affirm the lower court’s finding on appeal. MR 8.4; CA RPC Rule 8.4. MR 5.2; CA RPC Rule 5.2. MR 8.5; CA RPC Rule 8.5. MR 8.3. CA RPC Rule 8.3. SBA § 6068(o).

rules.rule_statement: Rule A lawyer is subject to discipline for practicing in a jurisdiction where he is not admitted. Lawyers are also obligated to ensure that only authorized persons engage in the practice of law and are subject to discipline for assisting nonlawyers in the unlicensed practice of law.

Rule A lawyer is subject to discipline for practicing in a jurisdiction where he is not admitted. Lawyers are also obligated to ensure that only authorized persons engage in the practice of law and are subject to discipline for assisting nonlawyers in the unlicensed practice of law.

rules.rule_statement: Except as permitted by a jurisdiction’s laws or ethics rules, a lawyer who is not admitted to practice in a jurisdiction cannot establish an office or other systematic and continuous presence in the jurisdiction for the practice of law or hold out to the public or otherwise represent that the lawyer is admitted to practice law in the jurisdiction. It is a misdemeanor to practice law in California without being a member of the State Bar of California or otherwise authorized by state law.

It is a misdemeanor to practice law in California without being a member of the State Bar of California or otherwise authorized by state law.

rules.canonical_name: Assisting Non-Lawyers In The Unauthorized Practice Of Law
rules.rule_statement: A lawyer must not knowingly assist a person who is not admitted to the bar in the unauthorized practice of law. The nonlawyer who engages in the unauthorized practice of law will be subject to civil and criminal penalties (e.g., in California, a misdemeanor ), but she is not subject to discipline under the ethics rules. A lawyer who engages in the unauthorized practice of law or assists a nonlawyer in unauthorized practice may be subject to civil and criminal penalties and is subject to discipline under the ethics rules.

The nonlawyer who engages in the unauthorized practice of law will be subject to civil and criminal penalties (e.g., in California, a misdemeanor ), but she is not subject to discipline under the ethics rules. A lawyer who engages in the unauthorized practice of law or assists a nonlawyer in unauthorized practice may be subject to civil and criminal penalties and is subject to discipline under the ethics rules.

rules.canonical_name: Employment Of Disbarred, Suspended, Resigned, Or Involuntarily Inactive Lawyer
rules.rule_statement: Under the California rules, a lawyer must not employ, associate in practice with, or assist a person the lawyer knows or reasonably should know is an ineligible person (i.e., a lawyer whose current status with the State Bar of California is disbarred, suspended, resigned, or involuntarily inactive) to perform the following on behalf of the lawyer’s client: Render legal consultation or advice to the client; ii. Appear on behalf of a client in any hearing or proceeding or before any judicial officer, arbitrator, mediator, court, public agency, referee, magistrate, commissioner, or hearing officer; iii. Appear as a representative of the client at a deposition or other discovery matter; iv. Negotiate or transact any matter for or on behalf of the client with third parties; Receive, disburse, or otherwise handle the client’s funds; or vi. Engage in activities that constitute the practice of law. This prohibition applies regardless of whether the ineligible person receives compensation.

rules.rule_statement: A California lawyer may employ, associate in practice with, or assist an ineligible person to perform research, drafting or clerical activities, including but not limited to: Legal work of a preparatory nature, such as legal research, the assemblage of data and other necessary information, drafting of pleadings, briefs, and other similar documents; ii. Direct communication with the client or third parties regarding matters such as scheduling, billing, updates, or confirmation of receipt or sending of correspondence and messages; or iii. Accompanying an active lawyer in attending a deposition or other discovery matter for the limited purpose of providing clerical assistance to the active lawyer who will appear as the representative of the client. The lawyer must serve written notice of the ineligible person’s employment upon the State Bar and each client on whose specific matter the ineligible person will work. A lawyer may, without client or State Bar notification, employ, associate in practice with, or assist an ineligible person whose sole function is to perform office physical plant or equipment maintenance, courier or delivery services, catering, reception, typing or transcription, or other similar support activities. MR 5.5(b); CA RPC 5.5(b). MR 5.5(c)(1). MR 5.5(a); CA RPC Rule 5.5(a). SBA § 6126. CA RPC Rule 5.3.1(a)(1)-(5), (b). CA RPC Rule 5.3.1(c). CA RPC Rule 5.3.1(d). CA RPC Rule 5.3.1(e).

rules.canonical_name: The Law Firm And Other Forms Of Practice
rules.rule_statement: A law firm is a lawyer or lawyers in a partnership, professional corporation, sole proprietorship, or other association authorized to practice law, or lawyers employed in a legal services organization or the legal department of a corporation or other organization. Whether two or more lawyers constitute a firm under the Model Rules depends on the specific facts. Therefore, if two practitioners share office space and occasionally consult or assist each other, they ordinarily would not be regarded as constituting a firm. However, if they hold themselves out to the public in a way that suggests that they are a firm or conduct themselves as a firm, then they would be regarded as a firm for the purposes of the Model Rules. The terms of any formal agreement between associated lawyers are relevant in determining whether they constitute a firm, as is evidence that they have mutual access to information concerning the clients they serve.
rules.short_rule_statement: A law firm is a lawyer or lawyers in a partnership, professional corporation, sole proprietorship, or other association authorized to practice law, or lawyers employed in a...
rules.source_text:
The Law Firm And Other Forms Of Practice
A law firm is a lawyer or lawyers in a partnership, professional corporation, sole proprietorship, or other association authorized to practice law, or lawyers employed in a legal services organization or the legal department of a corporation or other organization. Whether two or more lawyers constitute a firm under the Model Rules depends on the specific facts. Therefore, if two practitioners share office space and occasionally consult or assist each other, they ordinarily would not be regarded as constituting a firm. However, if they hold themselves out to the public in a way that suggests that they are a firm or conduct themselves as a firm, then they would be regarded as a firm for the purposes of the Model Rules. The terms of any formal agreement between associated lawyers are relevant in determining whether they constitute a firm, as is evidence that they have mutual access to information concerning the clients they serve.

rules.rule_statement: Attorneys in the same firm may share or split fees earned by any of them, by agreement. Fees may be shared with an attorney who has retired from the firm.

Attorneys in the same firm may share or split fees earned by any of them, by agreement. Fees may be shared with an attorney who has retired from the firm.

rules.rule_statement: California lawyers and law firms are prohibited from engaging in unlawful harassment, discrimination, or retaliation based on any protected characteristic in their client selection, representation of clients, and employment practices. “Protected characteristic” means race, religious creed, color, national origin, ancestry, physical disability, mental disability, medical condition, genetic information, marital status, sex, gender, gender identity, gender expression, sexual orientation, age, military and veteran status, or other category of discrimination prohibited by applicable law.
rules.short_rule_statement: California lawyers and law firms are prohibited from engaging in unlawful harassment, discrimination, or retaliation based on any protected characteristic in their client...
rules.source_text:
Prohibited Discrimination
California lawyers and law firms are prohibited from engaging in unlawful harassment, discrimination, or retaliation based on any protected characteristic in their client selection, representation of clients, and employment practices. “Protected characteristic” means race, religious creed, color, national origin, ancestry, physical disability, mental disability, medical condition, genetic information, marital status, sex, gender, gender identity, gender expression, sexual orientation, age, military and veteran status, or other category of discrimination prohibited by applicable law.

rules.rule_statement: A lawyer is not permitted to form a partnership with a nonlawyer if any of the activities of the partnership consist of the practice of law.
rules.short_rule_statement: A lawyer is not permitted to form a partnership with a nonlawyer if any of the activities of the partnership consist of the practice of law.
rules.source_text:
Partnership
A lawyer is not permitted to form a partnership with a nonlawyer if any of the activities of the partnership consist of the practice of law.
topic_path: The Law Firm And Other Forms Of Practice

rules.rule_statement: A lawyer is not permitted to practice with or in the form of a professional corporation or association authorized to practice law for a profit if: A nonlawyer owns any interest in the firm (except that a fiduciary representative of the estate of a lawyer may hold the stock or interest of the lawyer for a reasonable time during administration of his estate); ii. A nonlawyer is a corporate director or officer thereof or occupies a position of similar responsibility in any form of association other than a corporation; or iii. A nonlawyer has the right to direct or control the professional judgment of a lawyer. Example: Law Firm is organized as a professional corporation. It is improper for Manager, who is not a member of the Bar, to serve as the executive vice president of Law Firm. In California, law corporations are registered with and certified by the State Bar of California and operate under the Moscone- Knox Professional Corporation Act. Officers, directors, shareholders, and professional employees of a law corporation rendering legal services to the public must be active members of the State Bar of California. Law corporations are subject to the CA RPC to the same extent as individual lawyers.

components.content: Law-related services not distinct from legal services
components.source_text: 1)      Law-related services not distinct from legal services

rules.rule_statement: Lawyers may provide law-related services, defined by the Model Rules as services that might reasonably be performed in conjunction with and related to the provision of legal services and not prohibited as the unauthorized practice of law when provided by a non-lawyer. Law-related services include: providing title insurance, financial planning, accounting, trust services, real-estate counseling, legislative lobbying, economic analysis, social work, psychological counseling, tax preparation, and patent, medical, or environmental consulting. Under certain circumstances, the lawyer providing law-related services will be subject to the Model Rules, even though a nonlawyer performing those services would not. A lawyer will be subject to the Model Rules regarding the provision of law-related services if the lawyer provides services in circumstances that are not distinct from the lawyer’s provision of legal services to clients. Example: Lawyer assists Client with drafting a will and also provides financial planning advice as part of his estate-planning services. Lawyer will be subject to the Model Rules regarding both his legal services in drafting the will and his provision of law-related financial-planning services. A lawyer will be subject to the Model Rules while providing law-related services by an entity controlled by the lawyer individually or with others if the lawyer fails to take reasonable measures to assure that the client knows that the services are not legal services and that the protections of the lawyer-client relationship do not apply. The lawyer has the burden to show that reasonable measures were taken under the circumstances to communicate the desired understanding. Therefore, a sophisticated user of law-related services, such as a publicly held corporation, may require less explanation than someone unfamiliar with distinctions between legal services and law-related services.

rules.rule_statement: A lawyer or law firm may sell or purchase a law practice or area of a law practice, including goodwill if: iv. The seller ceases to practice law entirely or within a geographic area or jurisdiction; Written notice is given to the clients regarding the proposed sale, the clients’ right to seek other counsel, and the fact that consent to the transfer of files will be presumed if no action is taken by a client within 90 days; if a client cannot be given notice, then the representation of that client may be transferred to the purchaser only on entry of an order so authorizing by a court having jurisdiction; the seller may disclose to the court in camera information relating to the representation only to the extent necessary to obtain an order authorizing the transfer of a file; vi. The entire practice or area of practice is sold to one or more lawyers or law firms; and vii. Client fees are not increased because of the sale. The seller is also obligated to exercise competence in identifying a purchaser qualified to assume the practice.
